Regarding the `self` parameter in `signal` dummy methods: This is actually not as important as the method is (afaik) never bound to the class and even then it would never be called. How can we call pause if we are going to have it?
Two example forms provided below. Should be have both sync and async?
I guess sync only option is sufficient since it can be called everywhere.
Ideas?

```python
from curio import pause, apause

async def going_to_pause():
    pause()
    await something()  # Before executing this kernel pauses. Kernel.run() returns.
    await apause()        # Same as above.
```

Perhaps Popen is not the best example of this.   I've always viewed this feature as merely connecting two I/O streams together--not necessarily corresponding to a subprocess.  Maybe it's too much of a weird special case though.  I'm not sure.

This thread is the proof of how that "evil" writeable()
led kk, spoiled his style and time. I'd suggest
removing writable() and any other possible sync checks
from curio.  Trio may have them as it explores such concepts.

> Huh? If the developer loves his child tasks to much then he
> should join() them or await the coro in the first place.
> That's Curio Basics, CB 101.
> Do we still consider them as arguments for the issue?

This might be the crux of it really.  At the moment, you can be kind of sloppy in spawning tasks.  You can spawn them and never wait around for them.  Curio will still make sure they finish (provided they are non-daemonic).   Maybe it's better to force people to think about joining with the tasks if completion is important.

I can't make the function work as a decorator and as a context manager if it also has that optional keyword argument included.   If daemon is to come back, the context manager will have to be a different function.
Right now, there is no curio-native library so threads might be your best bet.    This is definitely something that I want to address in the future though.

Attached is a patch to fix the issue:
1. Ignore content-type, as it does not say anything about the existence of a body.
2. Do not attempt to read body if response status code is 1xx, 204 or 304.
3. Here comes the ugly part: ignore body if parameter head_response=True has been passed to Response object. HEAD responses are identical to GET responses (including Content-Length), except for the missing body. dpkt can not determine automatically whether this is a HEAD response.

Also attached modified dpkt_http_bug2.zip:
- dpkt_bug.py: call dpkt.http.Response(data, head_response=True)
- stream.txt: file ends with '\r\n\r\n' (instead of '\r\n')

**Attachment:** [http_fix_50.patch dpkt_http_bug2.zip](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=50#hc3)

I wholeheartedly agree with the first two bullet points in the previous comment, but I'm not sure about the third, purely because internally this could seriously mess up the inheritance structure of fitting objects in symfit.

This discussion actually ties in nicely with my desire to support vector valued functions at some point. (https://github.com/tBuLi/symfit/issues/15) Because for the data, it would also be nice if you where free to use whatever keyword you prefer:

```python
x, y = variables('x, y')
z = x**2 + y**2

fit = Fit(model=z, x=xdata, y=ydata, z=zdata)
```
vector valued example:
```python
x, y = variables('x, y')
z_1 = x**2 + y**2
z_2 = 2 * (x + y)
z = z1, z2  # This line would depend on which vector syntax becomes accepted. See issue #15.

fit = Fit(model=z, x=xdata, y=ydata, z_1=z1data, z_2=z2data)
```

For this to work, a model would also need to have a name. If the syntax as provided here should literally work, we would have to use inspect upon assignment to check the name you desire, which I would prefer not to do. Of course adding the line ```z.name = 'z'``` is not too much overhead but it still looks ridiculous. This would make more sense:

```python
# Scalar function
z = Model(z=x**2 + y**2)
# Vector function
z = Model(z_1=x**2 + y**2, z_2=2 * (x + y))

fit = Fit(model=z, x=xdata, y=ydata, z_1=z1data, z_2=z2data)
```

This way would be pretty much perfect, because now all the naming of components of the vector valued function is completely transparent and unambiguous.

There are only two drawbacks I can think of:
- these changes would break backwards compatibility of the API, forcing users to change their code. But since this would make debugging so much easier, I think it's a change people won't mind, and this is still a young project.
- In case of a simple scalar function, I would still prefer the current way of doing things: ```z = x**2 + y**2```. It just looks a lot cleaner than putting ```Model``` in everywhere. So I would prefer not to loose this way of doing things. Maybe a little inspect isn't bad?

Any thoughts?

So far I have found out that the generalised inverse of a matrix exists, and it's [generalised inverse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generalized_inverse) is the same as the normal inverse. So in that sense there is no harm in making this substitution.

However, there could be harm if the statistical interpretation of the two is not the same. So we would have to read about that in more detail. Other than that, it is very good to know, particularly because it always exists.

After playing around some more, I have found out that simply making `Variable`'s inheret from `IndexedBase` is not a viable solution for dealing with `ODEModel`'s, as `D(y, t)` rightfully throws `ValueError: Can't calculate 1st derivative wrt t.`

Solutions would be to force everyone to change their code to `D(y[i], t[i])`, bad idea and less readable, or to just have two types of `Variable`: the existing one, and a new indexed version.
```python
# Existing objects
class Argument(Expr):
    pass

class Variable(Argument, Symbol):
    pass

# New indexed counterparts
class IndexedArgument(Indexed):
    pass

class IndexedVariableBase(Argument, IndexedBase):
    pass

class IndexedVariable(IndexedArgument):
    pass
```
The same should happen for `Parameters`.

Apart from the syntax proposed above, we can also add a keyword argument `indexed` to the convenience methods:
```python
x, y = variables('x, y', indexed=True)
i, j = indices('i, j')
x_ij = x[i, j]
```

Depending on the background of the experiment the data would be from, either the data or the model is most important. When a model is already known and the experiment should fit or be discarded, i.e. the fit is proof the experiment did not yield usable results. Or the experiment is very new and no model is proposed yet, making the data more important than the fit.

With that in mind, it should always be clear to people reading the fit what the data is and what the variables would be. (The example here is quite clear since the data is called data, this might not be the case for all examples or users.) Therefore I would say that the use of keyword-arguments is preferable. Note then, that this is a consideration solely based on the experimentalist in me, not the programmer.

Additionally, in support now lives a function called `key2str`. You give it a dict, and it changes the keys of that dict to strings. In the example above,

```python
dependent_data = {var: self.data[var] for var in self.model.dependent_vars}
model(**key2str(dependent_data))
```
This means that such dicts can still be used to evaluate functions with relative ease, while making all other code _way_ better.

I know that the use-case you describe is a pain with FuseSoC today. I have been encountering it myself, but you know what... I have another approach for this problem. I was actually thinking about adding it as a final commit for v1.7, but decided against it because I want more testing.

My approach is to optionally just skip the export stage and reference the files from the original locations. This is also a behaviour that I think many people are actually expecting, so it would be a nice service to them as well. All the code is already in place, I just need to add a command-line switch (been planning to go with `--no-export`) and hook it up in main. The test suite already works in unexported mode for most of the test cases. If you want to try it yourself you can just change [this](https://github.com/olofk/fusesoc/blob/master/fusesoc/main.py#L208) to export=False and hopefully it should work.

The things that might break from this are (AFAIK)

* Extra include files might be picked up which FuseSoC normally ignores (like all the `timescale.v` files in many OpenCores cores) because they might have name conflicts
* Hard coded search paths

Other than that, I think it should be good.

The MIBs and the objects defined in MIBs are maintained by the `MibBuilder` by the MIB module + MIB name pair:

    mibName, objName = 'SNMP-FRAMEWORK-MIB', 'snmpEngineID'
    mibObj, = mibBuilder.importSymbols(mibName, objName)

Once you have the MIB object you can get all the information it has:

    print(mibObj.getDescription())

Additionally, the `MibViewController` maintains its own independent index by OID (as well as other helpful indices). So if you have an OID and want the description of associated object, you first need to ask `MibViewController` what's the name of the MIB object having this OID:

        mibName, objName, restOfOid = mibView.getNodeLocation(oid)

Then look up the MIB object:

    mibObj, = mibBuilder.importSymbols(mibName, objName)

And inspect it.

If you give it a longer OID (most commonly, the tail of the OID would refer to MIB object *instance* in SNMP terms), then that tail part would be returned as-is.

Ok, so update..

In regards to ListBox API.

Ignoring all the stuff which ListBox will inherit from TextWidget (`bg`, `text_color`, `font`, `after`, etc, etc) it will have the following properties and methods.

+ `__init__(self, master, items=None, selected=None, command=None, args = [], grid=None, align=None, visible=True, enabled=None, multiselect=False)`
  + master - container the widget is 'owned' by
  + items - a list of string for items to add into the ListBox
  + selected - item or items to select at init
  + command - a function which will get called when the ListBox selection changes
  + args - a list of arguments to pass to the command
  + grid - grid position
  + align - alignment in grid
  + visible - if visible at init
  + enabled = if enabled at init
  + multiselect - if the ListBox should allow multiple items to be selected
+ `value` - Sets or returns the items selected in a ListBox `None` if 0 items are selected. If the ListBox is a `multiselect`, `value` is a tuple of items selected, if not `value` is a single integer.
+ `insert(index, item)` - Insert a new `item` at `index`.
+ `append(item)` - Appends a new `item` to the end of the ListBox.
+ `remove(item)` - Removes an `item` from the ListBox.
+ `clear()` - Clears all the items from a ListBox
+ `items` - Returns a list of items in the ListBox
Now all widgets support the same set of methods and properties, I think I can create an class hierarchy...  Happy days,

Whenever going through a terminal server with reverse telnet to log into the console port of an end device which, has also been configured to ask for authentication on that port, we will have to go through 2 authentication interactions:
The first user/pass prompt comes from the terminal server and the second user/pass prompt comes from the end device we are trying to get access to.

As it is today in 1.4.2, netmiko with “generic_termserver_telnet” will call the “telnet_login” which inserts the same username and password every time “read_channel” comes back with text that matches “username_pattern” and “pwd_pattern”.
The login method will not return a success response until a device prompt terminated by either “>” or “#” is received. 

The issue is that not every OS/vendor asks for [uU]sername by default.
If, for example, the terminal server is an ios device but the target device is a junos, then netmiko will go through the first authentication prompt (because cisco will ask for “Username:”) but will fail to login to the Juniper device since it will send back a “login:” a prompt. The “telnet_login” method will be stuck in a loop for the default “max_loops=60” and finally return a “Telnet login failed:” message even though it had successfully passed the terminal server authentication.

Example:
Laptop --GigEth--> cisco 2901 acting as terminal server --Serial RS232--> Juniper QFX5100 target device
Let’s say the QFX5100 is connected to the first Async port on the 2901: port 2002
To get access to the QFX5100 we use the 2901 IP address visible to the laptop: 10.0.0.1
So, to get access to the QFX5100 console port we issue: telnet 10.0.0.1 2002
First prompt will be the cisco with “Username:/Password:” netmiko will pass this and continue to look for >/#
Second prompt will be the Juniper with “login:/password:” netmiko will fail this because login does not match the username_pattern =r“sername”

With this pull request I’m proposing we extend the possibilities of the username_pattern to  include “[uU]ser:” and “[lL]ogin” which should catch a most scenarios.
Tested successfully in a couple scenarios since yesterday with different devices: Quanta Lb9 Fastpath, QFX5100 junos, c2901 ios, Accton with cumulus

Where I see this breaking: 
Some terminal servers like the Avocent models get you a message like this after the first authentication:
“Type the hot key to suspend the connection: <CTRL>Z”
Because the of the “>” in the phrase above netmiko thinks it’s a router prompt and start sending the show commands before it actually logs into the end device.
To fix this we, would have to change the pri_prompt_terminator='#', alt_prompt_terminator='>' to a regex search for r’>$|#$’.

Spyder uses Jedi for autocomplete, and Jupyter has recently integrated it too. But:

1. Relevant information is not always available statically (e.g., your editor won't know what type `cd.shared.foo` is, and even if you know that a variable is a DataFrame, Pandas' `dataframe.columnname` is very a useful autocomplete that relies on dynamic information).
2. Often I'll want to have a quick look at some intermediate value (e.g., the shape of an ndarray) to make sure that things look right, then continue on. I'll either poke at something in Spyder's interactive console, have a quick look at its variables view, or make a one-line cell in the code editor to re-run in quick iteration.

Even these aren't anywhere close to the full live-programming vision that Victor and others have, but they're vaguely in the right direction.

The biggest problem with these more "live" flows is the "convergent" phase: after you've played with stuff to figure out what you want to do, then you want to clean it up into something reusable, intelligible, and reliable. When I saw Cauldron's shared variables and clean documentation abilities, I realized you might be on to something in that direction. But though code should _end up_ that organized, it shouldn't _start_ that organized. Cauldron has a real opportunity to innovate in how it helps you incrementally bring structure to your data science chaos.

For other design inspiration, see [JupyterLab,](https://jupyterlab-tutorial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) and [d3.express](https://medium.com/@mbostock/a-better-way-to-code-2b1d2876a3a0), and also [Spyder](https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/).

hth,

I fixed this for my site using the following simplistic middleware:

import re
from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse

class RebaseLinks(object):

    def process_response(self, proxy, request, upstream_response, response):
        # change only html responses
        if 'text/html' not in response['Content-Type']:
            return response

        # (?:<<regex>>) signifies non-capturing group
        response.content = re.sub(r'''((?:src|href)=['|"]?)(/)''', r'\1'
                                  + reverse(request.resolver_match.url_name, kwargs={'url': ''}) + r'\2',
                                  response.content, flags=re.IGNORECASE)

        return response

This is very generic and could be dropped-in to any proxy that uses a prefix.

`color66` isn't a typo for `color16`.

`color66` is an additional color that is set to the value of `color0`. When you try to use `color0` and bold text is enabled in your terminal `color8` is used instead. This means that instead of getting a black background or text, you're left with a light grey color instead.

The bug here is that `color66` shouldn't end up in the export files.

Ah I see what you mean now. I don't think that is a good idea.
It would require maintaining a hard-coded version tuple in each C extension module, which is a risky extra effort because it's easy to forget to update all the tuples on every new release.
Also, the existing users will have an old version of psutil where such a tuple does not exist, which would turn our code into something like this:

     if not hasattr(_psplatform, "version") or _psplatform.version != __version__:
          ....

To me that looks too hackish and probably not robust enough.
The use case you describe is pretty twisted, in fact I've never bumped into something like this.
What you say is going on is that "import psutil" imports the more recent *.py modules (installed via virtualenv+pip) but the old *.c extension modules installed at system level (installed via apt).
That clearly means there's something wrong with the virtualenv or someone played with sys.path in ways he/she was not supposed to. I can't think of any robust way to detect such a messed up environment and raise an error at import time rather than later.
I would suggest to either fix your build system or include a routine which checks such an unusual scenario into your own code rather than expecting psutil to perform such a check.

The simple explanation is that the ssd1306 implementation does not make use of the different framebuffers, and instead just does a full frame render (and ignores any args it does not care about).

The diff_to_previous framebuffer was an attempt to make devices with color and/or higher resolutions perform better. It has been implemented for:
* ST7735
* SSD1331
* SSD1322

In theory, it _should_ be simple to add the same capabilities to SSD1306, SH1106 and SSD1325... I had implemented the same logic for SSD1325 but the way the memory is mapped in the device made it awkward so I ended up not committing it in the end. I suspect that this might be a similar trapdoor for SSD1306.

Interestingly, on my Orange Pi Zero (same kernel version as yours), I am seeing frame rates of ~20FPS with python3 ... previously (Dec 2016?) I'm pretty certain it was hitting somewhere around 55FPS on python2 on OPi0. Hmmm.... 🤔

1. Data is not extracted from textual feed.
2. Use `nflgame.live` or even better, `nfldb`.
3. No. The "feed" is provided by NFL.com. Except it's not a "feed." It's an undocumented internal API that I reverse engineered.

With that said, configuring where JSON data is cached may be a worthwhile endeavor. The data is growing every year, so the Python package directory may not be the best place to put it. Other Python packages that have a similar problem are `nltk`, but I've always found their approach annoying. Basically, you have to explicitly download the nltk data to directory, and if you don't, some of nltk's functionality won't work. The problem with that approach for us is that nflgame will work just fine without a cache (because, you know, it really is a cache). Which means it would be very easy for users to completely neglect downloading data, and we'd all end up hitting NFL.com way too much. So... some more thought needs to go into this.

Hi @ericbhanson ,

After some serious consideration, I decide not to merge this change.  Reasons are listed below:
* You could achieve your goal by doing following settings before getting any stats:
``` python
  StockDataFrame.BOLL_PERIOD = 10   # or other values you need
  StockDataFrame.BOLL_STD_TIMES = 2.5
```
  After you change them, the new values will be used for calculation.
* Your change requires to change the column name from `boll_ub` to `boll-ub`.  For backward compatibility reasons, I don't want to touch the name of the stats used very often.
* The new function which requires 2 more parameters actually cannot be used in the current column name parsing structure.  It only supports one window.  And add a window in the column name would require to change the name from `boll_ub` to `boll-ub`.  What's more, you will still get a attributes not found error because you actually initialize the `2.5_10_boll-ub` column but not the `boll-ub` column.  In order to initialize the column name specified by user, we need a way to pass the column name down to the calculation function, but it's not doable in current structure.

Anyway, thanks for the contribution and let me know if you have more requirements.  I will try to add them in the lib if I could 😄

You can't pass a Python list as a SQL parameter.  The parameters have to correspond to single values - strings, integers, floats, datetime.date, etc.  See the list of available object types on the wiki page https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Data-Types , look for the "Python Datatype" column in the first table there.

> That means that I would have to do two connections: one to drop the table in the destination access file and the other to run the creation query.

Actually, no, the Access Database Engine lets us use a connection to one database and manipulate tables in another database, even if those tables are not explicitly linked. Note that in the example below I connect to "Database1.accdb" and then drop a table in "OtherDatabase.accdb":

```python
conn_str = (
    r'DRIVER={Microsoft Access Driver (*.mdb, *.accdb)};'
    r'DBQ=C:\Users\Public\Database1.accdb;'
)
cnxn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str, autocommit=True)
crsr = cnxn.cursor()
try:
    crsr.execute(r"DROP TABLE [C:\Users\Public\OtherDatabase.accdb].movimientos")
except pyodbc.ProgrammingError as pe:
    if pe.args[0] != "42S02":
        raise
crsr.execute("{CALL MovimientosWeb}")  # saved query in Database1.accdb
```

**TL;DR**: 

While not a bad idea, this isn't something that can be implemented very easily.  I'll leave this issue open for a while in case others want to chime in, but my initial reaction is that the added convenience in a few corner cases isn't worth the extra complexity and code it would require.

**Long answer:**

FanFicfare can find the number of 'chapters' in existing epubs by counting the chapter files.  There isn't any standard way to retrieve the existing word count.  While it may be present in title page, log page, or calibre custom columns, those are all configurable options that can vary from user to user.  I've investigated and even written code before to store all of FFF's metadata inside the epub, but there are too many ways for it to be discarded during other processing.  Plus, there are sites that don't report word counts at all.

So preventing update based on the number of new words is problematic.

Another possible option is to look at the length of 'new' content _after_ downloading and abort the update if it's under a certain limit.  

The network bandwidth and server time would still be expended, but it's somewhat more doable.  Word count would only be a rough estimate due to stripping HTML issues (some people get entirely too worked up about exact word counts).  Because it would be done after downloading, the calibre PI version would not be able to stop and ask the user to accept or reject the update.  And if you started reading a story of short chapters (100 word 'drabble' stories are a thing I've seen), you'd need to either change your settings or set specific lower limits for those stories.

(You might also ask your authors to stop posting non-chapters--as I recall, it's forbidden by the site terms on the most popular sites.  It's also pretty easy to delete a chapter from an epub using Calibre's Edit Book.)

Your last example does have 'choices'. The only valid thing a user can type is literally ```option``` because that is the name of your subparser. This can be easily visualized by running the example and letting argparse do its job:

```
$ python test.py 
usage: test.py [-h] {option} ...
test.py: error: too few arguments

$ python test.py foobar
usage: test.py [-h] {option} ...
test.py: error: invalid choice: 'foobar' (choose from 'option')
```

In general you can't expect argcomplete to complete stuff that it doesn't know the valid values for. In the first example the only valid value is ```---option``` in the third it's ```option```. But the positional argument in the second example will accept any user-supplied value and therefor can't be completed.

Thanks @tomerfiliba. After looking at [the virtualenv documentation for the activate script](https://virtualenv.readthedocs.org/en/latest/userguide.html#activate-script), it seems the only necessary alteration was inserting the virtualenv's `bin/` directory as the first entry of the `PATH` environmental variable. I arrived at the following

```python
@contextmanager
def virtualenv(virtualenv_dir):
    """A context manager for executing commands within the context of a
    Python virtualenv.

    >>> with virtualenv('/path/to/virtualenv'):
    ...     print(local.python("-c", "import sys;
                               print(sys.executable)"))
    ... #...

    """
    old_path = local.env['PATH']
    virtualenv_bin_dir = (Path(virtualenv_dir) / 'bin').resolve()
    new_path = '{}:{}'.format(str(virtualenv_bin_dir), old_path)
    local.env['PATH'] = new_path
    old_python = local.python
    new_python = local['python']
    local.python = new_python
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        local.env['PATH'] = old_path
        local.python = old_python
```

It seems to work suitably.

One concern I have is that this is probably not parallel-safe. That is, this change is global and affects all operations using the `local` object. This becomes a concern to me when I use plumbum in the context of [doit](http://pydoit.org/), which can execute tasks in parallel. If one task uses this context manager, it will change the `PATH` environmental variable and the `python` executable for all currently executing tasks. @tomerfiliba, do you have any suggestions on creating a version of this context manager that is parallel-safe (i.e., does not change global state)?

I've been thinking about it, and there is 3 ways to deal with an async sequence of async subsequences:
- **concat**: yield items from subsequences in strict order
- **flat**: yield items from subsequences as soon as they arrive
- **switch**: yield items from the latest subsequence

They all have their corresponding map operator: **concatmap**, **flatmap**, **switchmap**.

Now, in the case of a sync sequence of async subsequences:
- **concat** becomes **chain** and the subsequences are given as arguments
- **flat** becomes **merge** and the subsequences are given as arguments
- **switch** doesn't make sense in this case (it would switch directly to the latest argument)

Now two questions need to be answered:
- Is this wording acceptable? Is it going to be confusing?
- Should **concat**, **flat** and **switched** be exposed, or remain internal operators?

@dmzkrsk What do you think?

this is an issue other users have had (see #188) and it basically means that the version of ssl your python is using is too old to understand the newer JPL TLS 1.2 certificate for the https connection. This actually was a common issue among other open source projects over the past few months so by now if you google `python TLS 1.2` a lot of stuff should pop up. This is particularly problematic on Macs.

This link has a decent explaination of the issue, but without a fix: https://pyfound.blogspot.com/2017/01/time-to-upgrade-your-python-tls-v12.html

So there isn't really anything to fix with urllib, but I wish I could find an exact solution for people to update their systems. In any case downloading cspice manually is the way around this if upgrading ssl/openssl is not an option.

I solve the Management Form issue in what I believe is a more elegant way, using a template tag to render it.

```@register.simple_tag
def djng_formset_management_form(formset):
    management_form = formset.management_form
    scope_prefix = formset.form.scope_prefix
    for name, field in management_form.fields.iteritems():
        field.widget.attrs['ng-model'] = ("%s['%s-%s']" % (scope_prefix, formset.prefix, name)) if scope_prefix else \
            '%s-%s' % (formset.prefix, name)
    return management_form```

telegram-send --config group1.conf --configure-group

telegram-send --config group1.conf "hello!"

Or to the other group:

telegram-send --config group2.conf "hello!"

I can't explain it any differently from what I tried  above, and Wikipedia also says about audio normalization that it is:

> the application of a constant amount of gain to an audio recording to bring the average or peak amplitude to a target level (the norm). Because the same amount of gain is applied across the entire recording, the signal-to-noise ratio and relative dynamics are unchanged.

Thus, your raw signal is not multiplied by a constant, but you add or subtract to the signal values whatever is needed to change the overall dB level by that constant (based on the logarithmic relationship between dBFS level and sample value).

The difference between peak and RMS normalization is not how that constant is calculated but which volume target you are trying to hit in the end.

thanks for doing this; I think further optimizations can be made, and allow
using this image both in the "old" way, and in the new way.

First of all, you can add a `.dockerignore` file to save some bytes (for
example, the Dockerfile, and `.git` directory don't have to end up in the
image, and only take up space). Something like this:

```gitignore
.git
.gitignore
.dockerignore
.Dockerfile
README.md
butterfly.png
```

The Dockerfile can save quite some space by combining some steps,
and cleaning up unneeded cache files. Also (as explained above), if
the `run.sh` has the right permissions in the Git repository, then
the `chmod` step can be skipped;

```Dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:14.04.4

RUN apt-get update \
 && apt-get install -y -q --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential \
    libffi-dev \
    libssl-dev \
    python-dev \
    python-setuptools \
 && apt-get clean \
 && rm -r /var/lib/apt/lists/*
 
COPY . /opt/app
WORKDIR /opt/app

RUN python setup.py build \
 && python setup.py install

EXPOSE 57575

CMD ["butterfly.server.py", "--unsecure", "--host=0.0.0.0"]
ENTRYPOINT ["docker/run.sh"]
```

Changing the Dockerfile to the above makes the image roughly 30MB smaller;

```
REPOSITORY         TAG     IMAGE ID            CREATED             SIZE
butterfly-after    latest  2df8600c2ca8        14 minutes ago      381 MB
butterfly-before   latest  bc7ecf560435        2 hours ago         409.8 MB
```

changing the `run.sh` to;

```bash
#!/bin/bash -e

# if command starts with an option, prepend the default command and options
if [ "${1:0:1}" = '-' ]; then
  set -- butterfly.server.py --unsecure --host=0.0.0.0 "$@"
fi

# Set password
echo "root:${PASSWORD:-password}" | chpasswd

exec "$@"
```

Allows you to run the image both the "old" way, and the "new" way, and you can still
override the default command (so, for example `docker run -it garland/butterfly bash` also works),
as do:

```
docker run -d  garland/butterfly

docker run -d  garland/butterfly --login

docker run -d  garland/butterfly /opt/app/butterfly.server.py --unsecure --host=0.0.0.0 --login
```

The image could probably be made a lot smaller still if alpine is used as a base (but I didn't try yet)

Note that when you are setting the `gateways` field; **assh** will iterate over all the entries of this field until the the first successful connection.
So technically, the first host is not a fallback, but the second one, the third one... are

If you want to have "fallback gateways", you need to put the magic "direct" host as the first entry of this field, so **assh** will try to connect without gateways before trying the rest of the list.

Do you find that odd? I may write a note on the README.md if this is not 100% clear as it is for me the feature I'm using the most every days :)

---

**example:**

```yaml
hosts:
  aaa:
    gateways:
    - bbb
    - ccc
    - direct
    - eee
```

`ssh aaa` will try until first success:
* `ssh bbb` -> `ssh aaa`
* `ssh ccc` -> `ssh aaa`
* `ssh aaa`
* `ssh eee` -> `ssh aaa`

Please find below script to do digital makeup for real time. Currently, I'm using PIL. If we can change to opencv it will be much faster. At least for landmark, it is fast compared to PIL.

For faster performance make sure to add **down-scaling**.
```
down_sampling = True
```

**Code**:
```
import cv2
import face_recognition
from PIL import Image, ImageDraw
import numpy as np


def PIL2array(img):
    """ Convert a PIL/Pillow image to a numpy array """
    return np.array(img.getdata(),
        np.uint8).reshape(img.size[1], img.size[0], 3)

def up_sample(landmark_list , sample_size=4):
    for face_landmark in landmark_list:
        if len(face_landmark) > 1:
            for key in face_landmark.keys():
                face_landmark[key] = [(w[0]*sample_size , w[1]*sample_size) for w in face_landmark[key]]
    return landmark_list


class FaceLandMarkDetection:

    def predict(self , frame):
        face_landmarks = face_recognition.face_landmarks(frame)
        if down_sampling:
            self.face_landmarks = up_sample(face_landmarks)
        else:
            self.face_landmarks = face_landmarks
            
            
    def plot(self , frame):
        pil_image = Image.fromarray(frame)
        for face_landmarks in self.face_landmarks:
            if len(face_landmarks) > 1:
                d = ImageDraw.Draw(pil_image, 'RGBA')

                # Make the eyebrows into a nightmare
                d.polygon(face_landmarks['left_eyebrow'], fill=(68, 54, 39, 128))
                d.polygon(face_landmarks['right_eyebrow'], fill=(68, 54, 39, 128))
                d.line(face_landmarks['left_eyebrow'], fill=(68, 54, 39, 150), width=5)
                d.line(face_landmarks['right_eyebrow'], fill=(68, 54, 39, 150), width=5)

                # Gloss the lips
                d.polygon(face_landmarks['top_lip'], fill=(150, 0, 0, 128))
                d.polygon(face_landmarks['bottom_lip'], fill=(150, 0, 0, 128))
                d.line(face_landmarks['top_lip'], fill=(150, 0, 0, 64), width=8)
                d.line(face_landmarks['bottom_lip'], fill=(150, 0, 0, 64), width=8)

                # Sparkle the eyes
                d.polygon(face_landmarks['left_eye'], fill=(255, 255, 255, 30))
                d.polygon(face_landmarks['right_eye'], fill=(255, 255, 255, 30))

                # Apply some eyeliner
                d.line(face_landmarks['left_eye'] + [face_landmarks['left_eye'][0]], fill=(0, 0, 0, 110), width=6)
                d.line(face_landmarks['right_eye'] + [face_landmarks['right_eye'][0]], fill=(0, 0, 0, 110), width=6)
            
        return PIL2array(pil_image)

video_capture = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
video_capture.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_WIDTH, 1920)
video_capture.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_HEIGHT, 1080)


face_landmark_detection = FaceLandMarkDetection()
down_sampling = True
while True:
    # Grab a single frame of video
    ret, frame = video_capture.read()
    
    if down_sampling:
        small_frame = cv2.resize(frame, (0, 0), fx=0.25, fy=0.25)

        # Find all facial features in all the faces in the image
        face_landmark_detection.predict(small_frame)
    else:
        # Find all facial features in all the faces in the image
        face_landmark_detection.predict(frame)

    ### plot 
    frame = face_landmark_detection.plot(frame)
    
    # Display the resulting image
    cv2.imshow('Video', frame)

    # Hit 'q' on the keyboard to quit!
    if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
        break

# Release handle to the webcam
video_capture.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()
```

Hey...joining the conversation. I had a look at this and i think the problem is not the fallback as the screenshot above actually shows a native input which FF supports from [29+](http://caniuse.com/#feat=input-color).

It seems like you can't really have an empty color input value as the [spec](http://www.w3.org/TR/html-markup/input.color.html#input.color.attrs.value) omits any word on it and leaves this to the browsers. 
```html
<input type="color">
```
Chrome will give you "#000000" (requested in JS and submitted via POST). In IE11 it's an empty string (it doesn't support color input and handle the field as normal text input). I would guess FF follow Chrome on this.

The JS fallback just requests the current value and writes it to the input. Through the effect above this explicitly writes "#000000" into the input field for Chrome and FF, but "#FFFFFF" for IE as this is the colorpicker-library-default.

All in all: Seems like we can't have an empty value the easy way and may need to work around here in some clever way. But anyway i feel we should provide a browser independend default value.

Am i right on this @charettes? What do you think?

dir(x[0].author.refresh())

Word! sorry respond so late and to reopen but in reference to your comment...

This may help you or someone else that stumbles across this thread...

I have found it is super helpful to get the cmd line working and start inspecting the library (and flask object in general) especially if you have ipython running in your shell or better yet in an editor.  I prefer pycharm (free v avail) which will do just about everything you need all in one app including running shells , ssh, database (depending on if u pay) and deployment.  This will also allow you to browse your installed site-packages in a tree as well.  There is no better documentation than the package itself. :)

When you manage to get it running in a shell take a look at the `cls.__dict__` methods of the flask objects to see what all the configs and settings are of flask-security (FS) module and current_app (if you want to go crazy check out current_user too) as well as your current state and other important values.

example: `print current_app.extensions['security'].__dict__`  

I like flask-script and there are some scripts in the current [master](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/script.py) that are not used unless you build out a manage.py(or alike) with flask-script.  There is also another fork using the click library for newer (3/2017) flask installs.

FS wraps several diff libraries which are sort of abstracted from view (Flask-Login, Flask-Mail etc) so getting in and seeing what the config values are is helpful.

Also what makes this library great is that it is well written and thought out.   You have the ability to over class bits and pieces of it to make it do exactly what you would like it to do to with your custom workflow without much effort or rewriting.   For example if you would like to add to or change the methods of the user datastore that is very easy to do by inheritance.
example:
``` from flask-security import SQLAlchemyUserDatastore
class ModDatastore(SQLAlchemyUserDatastore):
    def my_special_method(self):
        ....stuff....
    def get_user(self, **kwargs):
        print "I'm over-writing the get_user method"


..... ur app init .....
sec = Security()
sec.init_app(app, datastore=ModDatastore(db, user, role))


```

If you decide to do something like this i suggest getting very familiar with super() and what it does.  Its a very powerful tool when over/super classing. You can even super  `security.core.Security` or even the entire `security.core` and gain control of parts or the entire module.

Happy hunting 👍

I intended for `pyramid_services` to be used in the context of a pyramid application. The services themselves are reusable elsewhere but probably by some mechanism other than this library.

The definition of a "pyramid application" can be stretched into the domain of scripts/etc that do not have a request object via the use of the mechanisms documented above (thanks @aodag). For example, `pyramid.paster.bootstrap` gives you a valid request object that you can use as if you were in a view. If you are in a thread and don't want to run bootstrap there is also `pyramid.scripting.prepare`.

With this in mind I don't see a good reason to support a function that ignores the request. There's always a request in pyramid-land.

As far as `config.find_service` goes that's more interesting. It's problematic because it would only work after invoking `config.commit` but I'd be happy to add a directive that supports doing the query.

Consider this (actual real world) use case.

I have an API that is location dependent.  All results returned by the API depend on the geographical location reported by the user.  Rather than force developers working against my API to always provide lat/lng in their query strings, they can provide it in a single location in their code by setting a Location header on all their requests.

Being able to set ad-hoc headers in the same way that you can set an api_key for the Authorization header would be extremely useful.

Another use case for this would be APIs with polymorphic content based on the Accept header.  Developers testing my API should be able to set that header in order to specify their desired response type.

@kyleobrien91 I came across this issue and had to subclass `XMLParser` and override `_xml_convert` to solve it. I ended up with something like the following:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<root>
    <field name="attribute_a">121.0</field_a>
    <field id="0001" name="attribute_b">dasd</field_b>
</root>
```
Is parsed into:
```
[
   {'field': 121, 'name': 'attribute_a'},
   {'field': 'dasd', 'id': 0001, 'name': 'attribute_b'},
]
```
Unfortunately it doesn't work if there was an attribute to parse on the root element, as that attribute would be tacked onto the end of the list. But for my purposes that was fine.

@EdOverflow I don't think your methodology is correct because your sample size is very small and your timing measurements include unrelated and expensive operations such as allocating a `range` and calling `print`, which may also trigger garbage collections.

The following code demonstrates that the timing difference for 32-byte strings is about 2 nanoseconds:
```python
import time

N = 1000000000

token_1 = "000000000000000000000000000000000"
token_2 = "000000000000000000000000000000000"
token_3 = "100000000000000000000000000000000"

time1 = time.time()
for i in xrange(N):
    token_1 == token_2
time2 = time.time()
print str(10**9 * (time2 - time1) / N) + " ns per comparison for equal values"

time1 = time.time()
for i in xrange(N):
    token_1 == token_3
time2 = time.time()
print str(10**9 * (time2 - time1) / N) + " ns per comparison for unequal values"
```
That's about 0.1 nanoseconds per byte, which is comparable to Lawson & Taylor's 2010 results.

I agree that this is far from the cryptographic ideal, but it's also well below the amount that can be distinguished by practical timing attacks, so I think it would be reasonable to leave this code as-is. Python versions lacking `compare_digest` will soon be gone.

I think we should leave the tests as they are and there can be additional specific tests for the mkldnn backend. 

Our users should not have to care what backend is being selected and the behaviour should always be identical. This means that however I call a certain operator, row_sparse in this case, I would expect that I get a proper result in **any** case. It's great that the issue is already tracked, but I'm heavily opposed to removing that operator from our test suite. All operator tests have to be implementation independent and should not differentiate or exclude certain cases. From my point of view, this flaky test is only resolved when the mkldnn implementation has been fixed and when this test passes all the time. Removing it is not an option.

```
<tal:code define="code view/code" condition="code" replace="structure code"/>

That ended up in a maximum recursion error, because the tile was never correctly rendered as it doesn't had a html/head/body structure.

From a packaging perspective (and I'm not only talking about conda here), it is vastly better if each package has the ability to contain it's own config file (as opposed to having to alter a global config file using the install process).

When you step back and take a look at Python, this is the same way.  Python has locations it looks for modules (such as `site-packages`), and when you want to use one, it just looks for it (no global config which tells Python what all the modules available are).  This way, each Python package (whether it is conda, rpm, etc.) just has to contain what does into `site-packages`.

If methods named `_<trait>_default` are meant to be a public API, there's no reason they're respective underscores can't be removed, and an `@default` decorator added (it'd be tedious, but I wouldn't be opposed to making those edits to Jupyter/Ipython since it's a trivial change).

Outside of that option though, we could define a public method `HasTraits.trait_default_generator` that iterates over the class mro and accesses the appropriate private generator cache (`HasTraits._trait_default_generators`).

*note: I can see a potential use case for a metaclass setup step in which `BaseDescriptor` instances are allowed access to all the classes which have access to them, not just the ones that "own" them. In other words `DefaultEventHandler` instances could install the full range of default generators on a class so that iterating over the mro wouldn't be necessary. This would create more overhead when importing traitlets based modules however it would greatly simplify `TraitType._dynamic_default_callable`.

@burnpanck you can already specify 

```python
t = Dict(trait=Unicode(), 
         traits={
            'a': CaselessStrEnum(['foo', 'bar']),
            'b': CaselessStrEnum(['baz', 'bat']),
         })
```

`traits` is a dict of trait types specifying trait types of values for specific keys
`trait` is a  trait type specifying the trait type of all other values

At the moment we don't have such a functionality for keys. The reason is that in the context where we needed the constraint (widgets), dictionaries have to be json-serializable which already forces the keys to be of type string.

> If in my subclass chain DumpConfig occurred twice, I would have an error: ...

Inheritance bugs are easy to detect because they fail early, on development time.

> Here is a use case: ...

Regarding your case:
- the simple flag remains as is (it's the `show_config` trait), 
  and you may use it selectively, assuming that makes sense - you see, monkey-patched `start()` is invoked **only on the parent command**, not the sub-commands.
- The most flexible way would be to mix-in the `ShowConfigAndStop` at the `Jupyter` and `Ipython` subclass definitions.  You `SubApp` would not then be affected.  
  Is that too much of an effort to do, on development time?

> So the main problem solved by this PR is ...

No. Actually monkey-patching `start()` of the parent command is just one way to dump-configs.
Other times you may need to call dump-configs on the sub-command instance, not the parent-command.
Mixins allow for pluggable implementations, per command.

The only thing mensioned in JSONAPI specification (http://jsonapi.org/format/#fetching-filtering) is :

> Note: JSON API is agnostic about the strategies supported by a server. The
`filter` query parameter can be used as the basis for any number of filtering
strategies.

What you take about Filtering (http://jsonapi.org/recommendations/#filtering) is just a recommandation, it does not belong to the specification so ```flask-rest-jsonapi``` is really compliant with JSONAPI specification.

I don't use the recommandation of Filtering because it is a very poor strategie for filtering. Instead of that if you want to get the filtering stratagie mensioned in JSONAPI recommandation you can create your own data layer inherited from the default SQLAlchemy data layer and rewrite the filter_query method to do what you want.

I'd like this PR to be a step towards opentracing[1], so it might be nice to think about it in terms of a few types of functions:


- plain functions which do no magic at all:
    - `tracer.new_span("foo")`, tracer.new_child_span("foo", parent_id, ...)`
- functions which can pluck the data they need from thread-local, async, whatever context
    - `tracer.new_child_from_context("foo", my_context)`
- one function which magically does everything based on a globally configured context (or context factory)
    - `tracer.context_factory = AsyncContextFactory`
    - `tracer.trace("foo")`




[1]  - https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python

Yes. This is only partially documented in the `thrift.load` document right now, but basically you can do:

    # foo/bar.thrift

    import sys
    from tchannel import thrift

    sys.modules[__name__] = thrift.load(...)

This replaces the module 'foo.bar' with the generated module. Then other modules can simply do,

    from foo.bar import MyService, SomeType

If we go with reST, we need to decide how to document arguments. Unfortunately there is no single official standard for that, but at least [Google style](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html#Comments), [NumPy style]https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/HOWTO_DOCUMENT.rst.txt) and [Sphinx style](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/domains.html#python-signatures) are commonly used.

Sphinx style is used in Robot's code when documenting public APIs and it has been OK. It's a bit verbose and ugly, but a big benefit is that at least PyCharm recognized the syntax and can do code inspection based on the information. Google style looks a lot better, but we should test do IDEs support it the same way as Sphinx style.

We should also test how different argument specification styles work with Libdoc. It's possible that further Libdoc enhancements are needed.

    ${item_locator}=   Set Variable    ${menu_locator}//li[@role='option' and .='${item}']
    Click Element       ${menu_locator}
    Wait Until Element Is Visible    ${item_locator}   10

```

This tag line is basically synonymous with Kenneth. It may be obvious _now_ that software is for developers, but the reason the tag line exists is because a lot of software is written and designed without consideration for that fact. 

Pipenv is not unlike requests— it’s a bit more opinionated, but it’s in a more controversial space. We target the use case of someone who comes from another language like JavaScript or PHP and is accustomed to a lockfile and an environment manager all in one. We also target the new developer who doesn’t know about virtualenvs and for whom tracking and remembering activation, accidentally installing things system wide, losing track of dependencies, etc, pose significant challenges which have nothing to do with the problems they are looking to solve. By asking them to be familiar with many tools before they even begin developing, we have already created a significant amount of cognitive overhead which can be a turn-off. 

Obviously previous tools had many use cases in mind, and evolved with the ecosystem. This tool has the advantage of being new and unifying those tools with added functionality.

The distinction here is that we are interested in the user experience before added features. Unfortunately software doesn’t speak for itself, and when you have a brand and your goal is in line with it, sometimes it makes sense to use it.

Since it's much easier to add features than it is to remove them, I'd advise *any* new packaging system to start with a single file the way Rust has, and then use experience-driven-pain to decide whether or not they need anything more complex than that.

For the specific case you gave, it seems that Rust has first class "example application" support built (http://doc.crates.io/manifest.html#examples) and otherwise requires that applications and libraries by laid out differently (http://doc.crates.io/manifest.html#the-project-layout). It also has workspaces to more explicitly manage scenarios where projects are developed and published as multiple crates in a single repository: http://doc.crates.io/manifest.html#the-workspace-section

The big difference in Python is that we have two rather distinct problems to solve:

- how to provide an opinionated and coherent publisher-centric dependency management experience for folks starting a *new* project or actively changing the workflow for an existing one (i.e. `pipenv`)
- how to provide a consumer-centric "minimum required build bootstrapping capability" that can be readily incorporated into existing build and deployment tools (i.e. `pyproject.toml`)

As a new ecosystem, Rust had the luxury of imposing `Cargo.toml` and `Cargo.lock` as their publishing standard from the start, rather than Python's more complicated history of retrofitting first distutils (~1998), then setuptools (~2004), then virtualenv (~2007), then pip (~2011), and now pyproject.toml and pipenv (~2017).

After more deliberation with @kbourgoin offline, we're going to make the following changes from what I last gave:

1.  `streams` will no long be an argument to the `spec` class method.  There will instead be a class attribute called `outputs` (purposefully vague) that can be either:
   -  A list of strings, in which case this is assumed to be the field names for the default stream.
   -  A list of `Stream` objects

  I'll add some validation so people can't go crazy and mix and match.
2.  Names for arguments will be less verbose and more Pythonic.  This will lose some of the parity with the underlying Thrift classes, but honestly those are such a mess anyway (because there's no inheritance, so there are many little classes that all contain instances of each other), that this is not something we should really be subjecting our users to.  This means we'll have `parallelism` instead of `parallelism_hint`, `command` instead of `execution_command`, and `conf` instead of `json_conf` (and we'll convert it to JSON on our end automatically).

This leaves us with:

```python
class SuperComplexTopology(Topoology):
    multi_spout1 = MultiSpout.spec(name='multi_spout1', parallelism=4)
    simple_spout = SimpleSpout.spec(parallelism=2)
    batching_bolt = SomeBatchingBolt.spec(inputs=[multi_spout1, simple_spout],
                                          conf={'topology.tick.tuple.freq.secs': 1})
    directed_bolt = SomeDirectedBolt.spec(inputs={multi_spout1['direct']: Grouping.direct})
    perl_bolt = ShellBolt(command='perl', script='bolt.pl', 
                          outputs=outputs=[Stream(fields=['field1', 'field2'])]) \
                .spec(inputs={simple_spout: Grouping.fields('junk')})
    ruby_bolt = ShellBolt(command='ruby', script='bolt.rb', outputs=['foo']) \
                .spec(inputs={simple_spout: Grouping.fields('junk')})
    java_bolt = JavaBolt(full_class_name='com.parsely.yucky.YuckyJavaBolt', 
                         args_list=[45, 'arg2'], outputs=['coffee']) \
                .spec(inputs=[perl_bolt])
```

where `Stream`, `Topology`, `Grouping`, `JavaBolt`, and `ShellBolt` would be provided by the `streamparse.dsl` package, and `SimpleSpout`, `MultiSpout`, and `SomeBatchingBolt` have define the class attribute `outputs` like:

```python
class MultiSpout(Spout):
    outputs = [Stream(fields=['foo', 'bar']), 
               Stream(name='direct', fields=['dir1', 'dir2'], direct=True)]
    ...

class SimpleSpout(Spout):
    outputs = ['junk']
    ...

class SomeBatchingBolt(BatchingBolt):
    outputs = [Stream(['sum'])]
    ...
```

The only point of debate that I think might be left is how to handle `JavaBolt` and `ShellBolt`.  I kind of like the idea of making `spec` take the same arguments regardless of which class its for, for consistency, but I can also see the benefits getting rid of the `JavaBolt` and `ShellBolt` constructor and having a more concise DSL.  This would give us:


```python
class SuperComplexTopology(Topoology):
    multi_spout1 = MultiSpout.spec(name='multi_spout1', parallelism=4)
    simple_spout = SimpleSpout.spec(parallelism=2)
    batching_bolt = SomeBatchingBolt.spec(inputs=[multi_spout1, simple_spout],
                                          conf={'topology.tick.tuple.freq.secs': 1})
    directed_bolt = SomeDirectedBolt.spec(inputs={multi_spout1['direct']: Grouping.direct})
    perl_bolt = ShellBolt(command='perl', script='bolt.pl', 
                          outputs=outputs=[Stream(fields=['field1', 'field2'])],
                          inputs={simple_spout: Grouping.fields('junk')})
    ruby_bolt = ShellBolt(command='ruby', script='bolt.rb', outputs=['foo'],
                          inputs={simple_spout: Grouping.fields('junk')})
    java_bolt = JavaBolt(full_class_name='com.parsely.yucky.YuckyJavaBolt', 
                         args_list=[45, 'arg2'], outputs=['coffee'], inputs=[perl_bolt])
```

For now I'm going to proceed with everything assuming we want the first approach, where `spec` is the same for all of these classes, and `ShellBolt` and `JavaBolt` aren't weird for having an `outputs` argument to `spec` when none of the other classes can have that.  Any comments about it are welcome though.

New NumPy features we can now depend on of interest (not an exhaustive list, I just skimmed release notes):

- NumPy 1.8:
  - NaN-safe basic statistics functions added:``nanmean``, ``nanvar``, and ``nanstd`` 
  - `np.full` and `np.full_like` - safer to initialize than `np.empty` which fills with NaNs on some architectures
  - Advanced `np.newaxis` indexing: `array[np.newaxis, [0, 1]]` now a valid form.

- NumPy 1.9:
  - Additional NaN-safe basic statistics functions: `nanmedian` and `nanpercentile`
  - `np.percentile` has an `interplation=` kwarg to select several interpolation methods
  - np.median and np.percentile now support an `axis=` kwarg
  - np.median and np.percentile now have a `keepdims=bool` kwarg which will *retain* the axis in question as a singleton dimension for simpler broadcasting (**very useful**)
  - np.unique now exposes a `unique_counts` parameter

- NumPy 1.10:
  - NaN-safe product: `nanprod`
  - `diagonal` and `diag` functions now return *writeable* views.
  - `np.linalg.multi_dot` added for brevity in linear algebra expressions
  - `np.stack` - combining arrays along a new axis (**lots of potential** to replace `np.concatenate`)
  - `np.cbrt` fast cube roots
  - `np.fft` new `norm=` kwarg potentially of interest

- NumPy 1.11:
  - `np.histogram` has automated bin size estimation available with `bins='auto'` and several specific methods
  - `np.moveaxis` for more control over axis swapping
  - **`SkipTest` and `KnownFailureException` exception classes are exposed in the `numpy.testing` namespace**
  - `np.gradient` now supports `axis=` kwarg
  - `np.testing.assert_warns` can now be used as a context manager

Hmm, though this workaround seems to work, I suspect that it MUST be masking some deeper bug. The loop invariant here is:
  - There is exactly one entry in `starts` and one entry in `ends` for every open contour in `contours`. (Open contours are those for which `contour[0] != contour[-1]`.)
  - For each open contour, `contour[0]` is in `starts` and `contour[-1]` is in `ends`.

Plugging in a quick test that this invariant holds at each iteration would very rapidly pinpoint when things change that there is a contour that can't be indexed as `ends[contour[-1]]`. This would then be "the point where this entry was supposed to be added to `ends` in the first place" that Josh was looking for. My suspicion is either there's some floating-point-comparison bizarreness going on (though I'm not sure how that would work), or there's some degenerate case that I hadn't previously thought of. (E.g. where a contour makes some kind of lariat structure and gets closed too early? But I'm not sure how that would work either.)

Sorry I don't have time right now to sort this out in more detail myself, but I at least wanted to note my thoughts quickly. Probably this should be left as an open issue of some sort, even with the workaround in place. 

Also, note also that as symmetric as the code is, there's probably an equivalent issue with `del starts[head[0]]` on [_find_contours.py:182](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/measure/_find_contours.py#L182).

Doing denoise_bilateral on the *same image* array returns "denoised" which *sometimes* contains nans. Then applying reconstruction causes python to segfault:

denoised = denoise_bilateral(image, sigma_range=1, sigma_spatial=15)
seed = np.copy(denoised)
seed[1:-1, 1:-1] = denoised.max()
filled = reconstruction(seed, denoised, method='erosion')

The workaround is to use:
    seed[1:-1, 1:-1] = np.nanmax(denoised)

import numpy as np

Unapologetic breaking API change: `filters.gaussian` defaulted for some reason previously to `nearest`. SciPy defaults to `reflect`, which I agree with and argue should _in almost all cases_ be the default for algorithms which require padding.

This _bug_ in previous behavior resulted in numerous test errors. We don't want to keep subtly incorrect defaults around, so I strongly favor making this change.

I'm not sure I see the added security benefit of adding message signing in our setup. I can see that message signing in a more open system (i.e. one where clients/workers/brokers doesn't intimately know each other), can be used to add extra trust. However in our production setups RabbitMQ has a virtual host per instance, requires authentication, and firewall is blocking connections from anything but clients and workers.

That said, it's not I'm against adding message signing, but I would favour using a solution that uses our already existing shared secret key. I think that using public key signing just adds extra complexity in our setup due to the need for private/public keys and sharing the public keys (and if the same private/public key's are used for all involved parties, there's little difference to a shared secret key).

I aware that you are just using features already in Celery, but perhaps it's not too much work writing a task serializer that uses our secret key to sign messages.

-1 from me.

I think the common standard for imports in Zope (and Python in general) is to sort imports into three groups. First a group for imports from the standard library, a second group for imports from external dependencies and a third group for imports from the same project. This helps to make it clear where functionality is coming from and also helps in avoiding circular imports, as dependencies are imported before everything else. I also don't see the point of having a single import per line, and duplicating the prefix over and over again. Especially if those are backwards compatibility imports, so the chance of changing a single one of them and producing diff noise is low.

Taking AuthEncoding as an example, I don't mind the current state:
```
from AuthEncoding.AuthEncoding import (  # NOQA
    constant_time_compare, PasswordEncryptionScheme, registerScheme,
    listSchemes, SSHADigestScheme, SHADigestScheme, MySQLDigestScheme,
    pw_validate, is_encrypted, pw_encrypt, pw_encode)
```
If this would be non backwards-compatibility code, I'd write it as:
```
from AuthEncoding.AuthEncoding import (  # NOQA
    constant_time_compare,
    is_encrypted,
    listSchemes,
    MySQLDigestScheme,
    PasswordEncryptionScheme,
    pw_encode,
    pw_encrypt,
    pw_validate,
    registerScheme,
    SHADigestScheme,
    SSHADigestScheme,
)
```
This ensures adding / removing one of those lines results in a small diff file, without repeating the same prefix.

This PR also comments out some unused imports. But these might be used by other packages or other places in the code. I'd rather keep them with a NOQA marker as before or put in a proper zope.deferredimport deprecation.

On a more general note, I think isort is dangerous, as there are too many places in Zope where import order matters and circular import problems are common.

A quick fix is below, porting from the source, paring it down and making a few slight tweaks.

I believe the issue is with the body returned by `requests.get()` and reading of the resulting bytes.  (The traceback agrees with this.)  For instance, try `data = requests.get(url).content` (gets bytes); this will fail.  Below, `data = requests.get(url).text` works.

```
import datetime
import requests
from io import StringIO
from pandas.io.common import urlencode
import pandas as pd

BASE = 'http://finance.google.com/finance/historical'


def get_params(symbol, start, end):
    params = {
        'q': symbol,
        'startdate': start.strftime('%Y/%m/%d'),
        'enddate': end.strftime('%Y/%m/%d'),
        'output': "csv"
    }
    return params


def build_url(symbol, start, end):
    params = get_params(symbol, start, end)
    return BASE + '?' + urlencode(params)


start = datetime.datetime(2010, 1, 1)
end = datetime.datetime.today()
sym = 'SPY'
url = build_url(sym, start, end)

data = requests.get(url).text
data = pd.read_csv(StringIO(data), index_col='Date', parse_dates=True)

print(data.head())

Joining the question: I want to get the zipcode of a USA location (given address or latlong).

With the python 3.6 changes to how certifications are handled, I thought I'd share my solution to having ssl problems with the geocoder example by monkey patching the `urlopen` attribute using the `certifi` certs:

Problematic code with no certifications:

```>>> from geopy.geocoders import Nominatim
>>> geolocator = Nominatim()
>>> location = geolocator.geocode("175 5th Avenue NYC")

<removed stack trace>
    page = requester(req, timeout=(timeout or self.timeout), **kwargs)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/urllib/request.py", line 223, in urlopen
urllib.error.URLError: <urlopen error [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:749)>
```

Here we pass the certifi certificates into the urlopen method:

```>>> from geopy.geocoders import Nominatim
>>> import certifi
>>> def uo(args, **kwargs):
...    return urllib.request.urlopen(args, cafile=certifi.where(), **kwargs)
... 
>>> geolocator = Nominatim()
>>> geolocator.urlopen = uo 
>>> location = geolocator.geocode("175 5th Avenue NYC")
>>> location.address
'Flatiron Building, 175, 5th Avenue, Flatiron, Manhattan, New York County, NYC, New York, 10010, United States of America'
```

Not sure if it helps this old issue, but that was the solution I came up with.

@sancar 

> Right now it wakes up every 100 millis. (1/10 of a second).

actually it does not, because the timeout is set to `0.01`, which is 1/100 of a second. asyncore calls this method ten times in a row, which means that asyncore itself does come back only after 1/10 of a second as you're saying. But it did ten syscalls in the meantime, whereas a single one would have the same effect here.

So setting count to 1 (or omitting it completely) and setting the timeout to `0.1` (which is what you're trying to achieve) would mean that you only use a single syscall instead of 10.

And well, the HZ client is already using asyncore, so it's integrated already :P

Okay, I think this one is ready for review now.

The main goal was to implement the geometric optics approximation to Fourier optics, which enables something like a 10^4-10^6 speed-up in photon-shooting optical and/or atmospheric PSFs (depending on source brightness and exposure time).  This approximation is now the default when drawing these objects using photon-shooting (and when drawing transformations, convolutions, etc. involving these objects).

I also made a few changes to the atmospheric API that I think make it easier to use.  For example, previously, the following would make two PSFs over two different time intervals:

```python
atm = galsim.Atmosphere(...)
psf1 = atm.makePSF(exptime=30.0, ...)
psf2 = atm.makePSF(exptime=30.0, ...)
```

I.e., `psf1` is exposing between t=0 and t=30 seconds, and `psf2` is exposing between t=30 and t=60 seconds.  After this PR, the above snippet makes both psfs expose from 0 to 30 seconds.  If you *want* 30 to 60 seconds, then you would do:

```python
psf3 = atm.makePSF(t0=30.0, exptime=30.0, ...)
```

Also new in this PR, the actual calculation for `psf1` and `psf2` above is delayed until either `psf1.drawImage()` or `psf2.drawImage()` is called (or `drawImage` for some object composed with `psf1` or `psf2` is called).  If drawing using Fourier methods, then drawing `psf1` will compute both `psf1` and `psf2`, which can sometimes be faster (and shouldn't be slower) when a boiling atmosphere is being used.

A few more changes:

- Changed signature of `wavefront()` method from `aper` to `u, v, t`.  I.e., instead of querying using an `Aperture` and using the "current time" of the screen, you query the locations and time of interest directly.
- Added `wavefront_gradient()` methods to screens and `PhaseScreenList`.
- Added `gradient` method to LookupTable2D.
- `OpticalScreen` now requires a `diam` be specified.  This is due to an API change for the `wavefront` method.
- Clock management methods (`advance()`, `reset()`) of phase screens and `PhaseScreenList` have been deprecated in favor of using the `t0` and `exptime` arguments when creating `PhaseScreenPSF`s.  (or when querying `wavefront()` or `wavefront_gradient()`.
- Deprecated the ability to return a list of PSFs from a single call to `makePSF`, since the delayed computation mentioned above makes it just as fast to call `makePSF` separately for each desired PSF.

Finally, I also added a new script: `examples/fft_vs_geom_movie.py`, which is a cleaned up and merged version of the scripts I used to make the movies below: 

- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l8d0jLVxBow
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H2LVXN7XT3c

Sorry for the long delay in replying here, other issues and projects got in the way of finishing this reply and summarizing my thoughts about your proposal to fix this from the outside.

----

If it turns out that there is a way to:

1. Reliably cache `setup_requires` dependencies
2. That will work for all users
3. Without major downsides

Then I'm all for it and don't mind spending time on implementing this. However I'm pretty sure things are not quite as simple as you explain it in your last comment, and this is the reason why I never seriously tackled this issue inside pip-accel before now:

* In most use cases of pip-accel the user runs either `pip-accel install PKG_NAME` or `pip-accel install -r REQ_FILE`. The way this works internally is that pip-accel asks pip to (1) download and (2) unpack the requested Python packages and (3) collect metadata about the packages. The first feedback pip-accel gets from pip after this is when step (3) has already happened. But step (3) is the step which invokes the installation of `setup_requires` packages. [Catch-22](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catch-22)! So properly speaking there is no place for me to put the proposed *"Please ensure this `.eggs` symbolic link exists"* logic.

  * For the above point to make sense you must realize that during a run of pip-accel we can't talk about a single "working directory" because every source distribution is unpacked to a separate directory and when pip runs `setup.py egg_info` it does so in the directory that contains the `setup.py` script. So during a single run of pip-accel we could be talking about a dozen "working directories" that each get their own `setup_requires` packages.

* As I said above properly speaking there is no way, but of course there is always a way, it's just that it might become somewhat nasty :-). I'd basically have to monkey patch pip so that pip-accel gets a chance to create the symbolic link after pip has unpacked the source distribution but before it runs the `python setup.py egg_info` command that triggers the installation of `setup_requires` packages. Looking into the pip source code it seems that I would have to monkey patch / override one or two of the below methods:

  * `pip.req.run_egg_info()`
  * `pip.req.req_install.run_egg_info()`

* Your proposal depends on the use of the `.eggs` directory which [was added in setuptools 7.0](https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/pull-request/97/cache-eggs-required-for-building-in-eggs/diff). Before that version setuptools would create `*.egg` directories in each working directory. Theoretically I could pro-actively link all of these `*.egg` directories for each unpacked source distribution, but I'm not going to do this because it's madness. So if I want this to work for all users I'd need to make sure that setuptools 7.0 or higher is installed. The natural way to do that is to just give pip-accel a `setuptools >= 0.7` dependency.

* If all of the above points are taken into account and this caching of `setup_requires` packages turns out to work, users would still be compiling `setup_requires` packages more times than strictly necessary because the setuptools `.eggs` cache and the pip-accel binary cache aren't shared, however of course this is still a lot better than recompiling setup requirements on every single run!

----

The best way for me to find out how realistic all of this is would be to (try to) implement the required changes. The issues caused by `setup_requires` have been a thorn in my side even before I created pip-accel and since then it hasn't gotten any better, so believe me when I say that I'd love to improve how this works :-).

However there is also issue #57 suggesting to upgrade from pip 6.x to pip 7.x and it seems wise to tackle that upgrade before I introduce yet more monkey patching (as explained above) because every additional pip monkey patch in pip-accel makes it a bit harder for me to upgrade to a new major version of pip.

I don't understand what is the problem with  conf.backup files: we don't overwrite existing configuration files in PGDATA, so the only case Patroni decides to copy over the conf.backup files is when the .conf files are actually missing from the PGDATA, but present in the backup: the latter means they were in the original PGDATA that were copied into the backup, therefore that PostgreSQL cluster likely used configuration files in PGDATA. If configuration files are stored outside of PGDATA - no con.backup files will be created.

WAL-E restore will be broken without copying/restoring the configuration files (as the replica created from WAL-E won't be able to start), so it should be turned on at least when wal-e is chosen as a replica method. We also symlink configuration files (we keep configuration files in git  for all PostgreSQL clusters in our data centre and link them to actual PGDATA) internally, so removal of this code will break Patroni for us.

Nevertheless, we can live with this functionality being optional, but I want to learn if there is an actual use case where it breaks the production system? Perhaps it would make sense to enable it automatically when WAL-E is set as a replication method, or when configuration files are symlinked?


For the conf.d, one can already point patroni to the existing postgresql.conf with the options described at https://github.com/zalando/patroni/issues/53. The only step is to create postgresql.conf, with include_dir pointing somewhere, but why should it be the task of Patroni?  A docker container containing the postgresql.conf with include_dir should be good enough to make sure the same configuration is propagated to the replica.

Incidentally, the purpose of hostPath in Kubernetes is to support network storage attached at the filesystem layer (e.g. SAN), not to allow you to use local storage for pods which the Kubernetes controller might decide to move around.  So I'd expect any setup involving using hostPath for local storage to eventually have problems.

As I understand, right now connexion only tries to validate JSON and nothing else (neither XML, which is possible but rather complex to add, nor anything else). The problem is, in some cases (see below) it also tries to validate other Content-types as JSON as well e.g. validate a text/plain against a JSON schema (hence the failing test I've added, it shows this). 

My proposal for validator is this:
* if response type is json (check response header), then validate against schema from spec.
* otherwise just pass as is.

It differs from current implementation, which is:
* Response content-type is not used at all,
* text/plain (from ```produces```) is treated as JSON and is validated against schema (https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/response.py#L75)
* when more than one ```produces``` items are present and not all of them are JSON, treat response as JSON (https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/response.py#L75) and validate it, even if returned type is not JSON.

As for checking if response Content-type corresponds to Accept header, I propose to not bother with it, as it's governed by HTTP standard, not Swagger standard and connexion is about Swagger. Same goes to weight factors.

Of course, this only relates to swagger 2. In Openapi 3 each content-type has its own schema and that requires its own thinking as well (leaving a question on how to validate a text/plain or text/csv against schema. Probably stick to "only validate JSON" solution for now).

BTW, there is a ```Produces``` decorator in ```decorators/produces.py``` that gets attached to a handler function and seemingly does nothing. What was it supposed to do?

        format: "int64"

        type: "integer"
        format: "int64"
      quantity:
        type: "integer"
        format: "int32"
      shipDate:
        type: "string"
        format: "date-time"
      status:
        type: "string"
        description: "Order Status"
        enum:
        - "placed"

        - "delivered"

        type: "boolean"

    xml:
      name: "Order"
