```
    type: "object"
    properties:
      id:
        type: "integer"
        format: "int64"
      name:
        type: "string"
    xml:
      name: "Category"
  User:
    type: "object"
    properties:
      id:
        type: "integer"
        format: "int64"
      username:
        type: "string"
      firstName:
        type: "string"
      lastName:
        type: "string"
      email:
        type: "string"
      password:
        type: "string"
      phone:
        type: "string"
      userStatus:
        type: "integer"
        format: "int32"
        description: "User Status"
    xml:
      name: "User"
  Tag:
    type: "object"
    properties:
      id:
        type: "integer"
        format: "int64"
      name:
        type: "string"
    xml:
      name: "Tag"
  Pet:
    type: "object"
    required:
    - "name"
    - "photoUrls"

        type: "integer"

    properties:

        type: "integer"
        format: "int32"
      type:
        type: "string"
      message:
        type: "string"
```

It is insane to try to make any changes when the tests are failing, we should focus on getting these tests stable first. I will try to do this over the weekend but help would be highly appreciated -- my time is quite limited.

The problem is with the test fixtures: because our tests are integration tests run against a cloud product it means that we do not start with a clean instance, it is likely to have leftovers from previous test executions. The trick is to improve the test fixtures to be able to cope with these left overs. Also we need to be sure that running them in parallel for each supported python version would not fail randomly tests.

I know that's much harder than classic unittests, but when we will have them working they will provide a much better QA than unittests because JIRA API is always changing and we do want to have a library that works with current versions of JIRA.

The way with how Chalice implements CORS is it will use the proxy integration with API Gateway. So the lambda function that Chalice creates will inject the CORS headers for you into the response that it returns and those headers will simply be proxied to API Gateway. Thus, API Gateway has no models related to the CORS headers because it is just acting as a proxy.

However for OPTIONS, there is no lambda function assigned for it and so it cannot rely on the proxy integration. Instead, Chalice has to rely on API Gateway modeling to include the CORS headers for the OPTIONS method and that is why you see it in the swagger document for the OPTIONS method.

Hopefully that makes sense. Out of curiosity, are you seeing the CORS headers when you make a sample response? They should be there. Or is there a particular reason you want the headers modeled instead of being passed in via the proxy integration?

I guess that if you want to run arm64 binaries e.g. on qemu, it is best to install package that will contain everything that is required (apart from interpreter you would also need specific arm64 libc.so and probably some more stuff).

For example, if you are using Ubuntu you can install `gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu` package from apt and then:

```
11:02:18 in ~/aarch_playground took 2s 
➜ aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc hello.c -o hello        

11:02:29 in ~/aarch_playground 
➜ qemu-aarch64 -L /usr/aarch64-linux-gnu ./hello
Hello world
```

The `-L ...` part is important - without it it will try to look for interpreter in `/lib/...` (I have used symlinks once for interpreter and it worked, but the more libs you use the more problematic things become).

* Py-ART follows the [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/) standard which can be checked using the [pycodestyle](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pycodestyle) tool.  There are a number of placed in this code that do not follow this convention.

``` Python
supported_file = bfile.endswith('.vol') or bfile.endswith('.azi') or bfile.endswith('.ele')

   raise ValueError('Only data files with extension .vol, .azi or .ele are supported')
```

``` Python
if field_name is None:
```

The spec is clear: no values can be omitted. The 'size' feature definition from Source Sans Pro works because the makeotf logic differs from the spec. In makeotf, if the subfamily id is 0, then the other design values are also set to zero, whether or not there are more values in the list. If the subfamily ID is supplied and is not zero, and there are fewer than 4 values, then the the missing parameter values are set to whatever garbage is in memory.

I think I prefer the makeotf logic (except for the bug) as it seems odd to make the user specify 3 zeroes instead of 1. This alternate form also makes it more obvious that the definition is not for  use with a size menu group. Anyone object if I change the feature file spec to match?

Fair enough. The AFDKO actually started out with several of the command-line programs wrapped as Python extensions. I stopped doing that because a) the minor Python versions kept changing, and the extensions had to be recompiled with each change, b) FontLab  had its own internal Python with different version numbers, and c) I had to do all the compiling and building, as the afdko wasn't opensource.  Now that the afdko can be hosted on github and the build process can be automated, I think it makes sense to go this way for the AFDKO. I am also against `from_kind`.

- A fast/easy onboarding experience is nice, but it's very difficult to use the datastore API without knowing what a key is. It's not our concept to hide, it's a core part of the API.
- How can a user know what a kind is without knowing what a key is?
- Adding a `from_kind` method adds another way to do something that is already possible
- The suggested `from_kind(...)` is the same (same-ish) number of keystrokes as using `Key(...)`.
- We can do creative things to make the constructor of `Entity` allow key construction. (See my "suggested middle-ground" above.)

OK, the problem is, one `p` and `Process` has been removed, is that when pickling the function `a`, pickle serialises the globals of `a`, and inside the globals of `a` is `a`. This means it stumbles upon its self, and pickle bales out. This doesn't happen in 3.4 due to https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/pickle.py#L603-L609 which for some reason was changed under PEP 3154. dill usually jumps in and helps out by changing the globals to a pointer to a modules globals, so there's no recursion, but since your evaling, the globals are not kept anywhere else.
I'm not quite sure how to fix this apart from moving to 3.4 yet, though, as its a kind of intrinsic pickle problem.

Ok, so you maximum sequence length is very long (256). Along with this batch size on a single GPU (even with 16GB) I doubt that this can fit.

A 'big' 6-layer transformer on 4 GPUs with word-based batch size and max-len 100 should probably be fine (I haven't tested this myself in a while).

If you have a machine with multiple GPUs (say 4), use `--device-ids -4` not `--device-ids 4` (the latter means 'use only GPU with id 4 - which wouldn't exist on a 4 GPU machine (0-indexed)).

I suggest reducing the batch size and/or the maximum sequence length.

It [appears to me][] that "Access-Control-Expose-Headers" is not meant to be set on preflight responses, but only on "actual" responses. SAM can't do anything with the application's responses; it's up to the application to set those. I think that adding it to a preflight response carries no meaning.

For our purposes, having a valid timezone name, as opposed to `local` which is not the valid name of a timezone, is more important than having the right timezone. Not having a valid timezone name makes Django fail to start, whereas defaulting to UTC means that we get slightly less accurate information about the particular timezone in which an interaction occurred, due to the particular system that it was setup in not exposing more useful timezone information.

Because we have very little control over the distributed server devices that run Kolibri, letting it run, but with a less informative default of UTC is our best fall back here.

There are some cases where we have more control over the environment, such as in the Android installer, where we can use a system specific API to ensure that this is set correctly before runtime, but in this particular edge case of an Ubuntu bash shell running on a Windows machine, we do not have that control.

I tried some stuff out, and I think we should try to move back to `classic` confinement.

Some issues with strict confinement that won't be fixed in the near future:
- File permission operations are blocked: `chown`, `chmod` etc fail. These operations are crucial for packaging Puppet dependencies, for example.

Hi Tim,

Thanks for getting back. Seen in hindsight I can see my description being a bit weak - I'll try to remedy that below with a more thorough example since what you propose doesn't completely solve the issue (although it's quite an improvement!).

The issue I’m trying to address is two-fold. The first problem is the one that the _personal file remedy which is that the custom configuration will be overwritten on each pull meaning the config will need to be redeployed at each update.

The second issue which still persist, is the fact that most python developers would leave the modules imported though pip outside the repo to keep the repo clean and not track modules outside the project, but instead just reference them in e.g. a requirements file and pull them every time you do a redeployment of the code in the repo. The consequence of this is, that if you update the configuration file (e.g. change the location from which credentials is pulled – in my case they are provided by a setup script and stored in a DB external to the application – or you setup an additional environment for people to use) you’re unable to push these changes to the config along with the rest of the updated code-base. This pose two issues: 1) you need to separate flows for deploying new code (one for the code + pip, one for the config) and 2) in case of a roll-back it’s not just rolling back to a specific commit but you need to dig up the old config-file and redeploy that though other means.

I propose two different solutions to this issue.

Solution 1) When you instantiate the SASsession there could be a parameter which will allow you to overwrite the location of the config-file so it can be kept somewhere in the repo and have a fallback to the “default” location of the config-file. This parameter would be used in the import-statement and with the personal and default config as fallback. 

Solution 2) The config-paramter should allow one to just provide a dict identical to the one found in the config-file. In that case it will skip looking in the config-file and use that instance instead. This could be decided based on if the parameter is a string (name of a config) or of type dict. This will require a not insignificant code-change across all config-objects to implement it properly as far as I can tell. I’ve managed to implement a quick-and-dirty fix with a relatively small code-change but I would recommend against taking that approach and rather re-think how the config-objects work to accommodate such a change in an efficient and clean manner.

Both of the above suggestions have the advantage of putting the config back in the code, allowing us to versionise it quite straight forward compared to having a bunch of symlinks and/or build-scripts that try to deal with this.

Let me know what you think and please don’t hesitate if it’s still a bit unclear.

The attack is:
.
1) I use a regular vanilla autobahn-python client to connect to ws://
evil.com/evil (unencrypted), through a dodgy intermediary (the kind that
the RFC is worried about, that is prone to cache poisoning attacks).
.
2) evil.com observes the first few masking keys that my client sends, and
uses them to infer the state of my RNG. (This is a well understood problem:
see e.g. https://github.com/fx5/not_random . And it looks like getrandbits
actually exposes the MT state very directly, making this step particularly
easy.)
.
3) evil.com predicts the next masking key that my client will use, and
sends a malicious PING request. The payload of the PING is one of the
pseudo-HTTP-requests described in the [TALKING] paper cited by RFC 6455,
pre-masked with my next masking key. For example: "GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost:
facebook.com\r\n\r\n". This is 38 bytes, so it fits easily into a PING
payload.
.
4) My client dutifully takes the PING payload, masks it -- this restoring
it to cleartext -- and sends it back as a PONG packet.
.
5) The server sends back a version of http://facebook.com that's been
altered to contain some arbitrary attack code.
.
6) The dodgy intermediary observes the exchange in steps (4) and (5), and
saves the altered version of http://facebook.com into its cache.
.
7) I or someone else behind the intermediary attempt to use our web browser
to visit http://facebook.com, and get the cached version delivered. Now
evil.com has arbitrary control over my Facebook account.
.
(Hopefully facebook actually uses HSTS, which should somewhat mitigate this
attack. But that's hardly a general solution; not every site uses HSTS, and
it doesn't help for first visits anyway.)
.
AFAICT this means that autobahn-python clients with predictable RNG outputs
are exactly as vulnerable to cache poisoning attacks as a browser with a
predictable RNG output would be. The scenario requires a few somewhat
unusual things -- use of unencrypted websockets, a malicious server, and a
dodgy intermediary -- but these are exactly the same requirements as the
browser version of the attack. The key insight is that the only thing JS
really gives an attacker is that it lets the server control the client's
outgoing plaintext, and websocket PING frames give the same capability
without JS.
.
The mitigation is simple and easy: just use os.urandom to choose masking
keys, at least over unencrypted connections. This doesn't need to be
particularly expensive.

I have some further thoughts on what's going on here, and where some of the confusion might be coming from (in case you aren't able to reproduce my error/issue on your machine).

This is actually related to pims, and how pims reads images using the ImageSequence object. In [image_sequence.py](https://github.com/soft-matter/pims/blob/155b01dca6941c4d56ee8f8b075f788ef092b2f5/pims/image_sequence.py#L24-L33) at the top, there are three different `imread()` functions imported. These each work differently. See [this gist](https://gist.github.com/charlesreid1/2087ab15d26a9eb07a13f896b2d7d626). The summary:

* `skimage.io.imread` does not normalize pixel values, so they are integers from 0-255
* `matplotlib.pyplot.imread` does normalize pixel values, so they are floats from 0-1
* `scipy.ndimage.imread` is deprecated in favor of `matplotlib.pyplot.imread` (as printed in the output of calls to scipy's `imread()` function), but it uses integer pixel values 0-255

I ran into problems with the mass values in the walkthrough notebook (and other notebooks) because I did not have scikit-image installed at the time I fixed the walkthrough notebook. However, after installing scikit-image (for the bubble foam notebook), I saw that all of the mass values had changed, because all of the pixel values were loaded differently.

I will need to update my pull request to use the *original* mass values that were there, now that I know this is not a change in the API but rather a missing package (scikit-image) on my end.

I also plan to open an issue in the pims repository about having inconsistent `imread()` behavior. It might be best to make either matplotlib or scikit-image a required package in pims, and whichever one is required, consistently use its `imread()` function. But that discussion should probably happen elsewhere...

Not quite - data isn't lost. And the monitors are what maintain quorum, not the daemons. Daemons store replicas of data.

The requirement for having multiple hosts, for Ceph or otherwise, is redundancy. Deis is a distributed system, and requiring three nodes is more or less hard requirement. Also, the SDK provides a convenience method for handling the the `id_token` so you don't need to mess with the JWT library directly.

Once you have the `cryptography` dependency installed (as discussed, `globus_sdk[jwt]`) you can call the `decode_id_token` function against a token response and get the contents of the `id_token`, e.g.:

```python
import globus_sdk

# ... set up and make a token request that includes an id_token

id_token = token_response.decode_id_token(globus_sdk.AuthClient())
```

[Link to the docs](http://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/optional_dependencies/#oidc-id-tokens)

You have raised a good point.  So far there is no way in the API to list all hosts.

Regarding connecting host and LUN, you could do this.

``` python
storage_group = vnx.get_sg(name="xxx")

storage_group.connect_host("host_name")
```
The host name specified here is the name you could see in VNX GUI.
I will work try to create a new API to retrieve all these names.

To connect a LUN to the host, add both host and LUN in the same Storage Group
``` python
storage_group.attach_alu(lun)
```

And I believe you could create a host with following API.
``` python
sg.connect_hba(port, hba_uid, host_name, host_ip=None)
```
If host name does not exist, VNX will try to create a new host.  If it already exists, the new path will be added to that host.
Port can be retrieved by following API.
``` python
vnx.get_fc_port()
vnx.get_iscsi_port()
```

Yes I assume that any sane Linux developer from the 21st century is on an OS that has access to `bash`, `awk`, and `ps`. None of these assumptions should be outlandish as mainstream systems come with these as a default since most system processes depend on one or more of those. Even on the HPC systems I work on where `csh` is the system/user default (legacy reasons), `bash` is still available to the user as is `ksh`, `zsh`, and `ash` (yay for pre-`dash`).

> It seems to me that all this complication could all be done away with by providing activate.tcsh and deactivate.tcsh scripts that use ($_) since tcsh seems to be the only shell that does not provide the minimum amount of compatibility/emulation with/of bash that would be required to launch a shell-specific script (on its own, without the need of bash and awk and ps). If other shells are similarly brain damaged then they need custom scripts too.

I am not sure what you mean by providing additional custom scripts...do you mean that `bash` users call `. activate` and `csh` users call ```source "`which activate.csh`"```...? Users can already do this with the current design, users can completely ignore the `activate`/`deactivate` top-level scripts that sniff the system and does the shell detection with the extra dependencies (`bash`, `awk`, and `ps`).

So for those users who for example only have `csh`, no `bash` or `awk` or `ps`, can simply activate by directly sourcing: ```source "`which activate.csh`"```. `bash` users can similarly just say `. activate.sh`.

And technically I wouldn't harp on any of the shells as being any more or less POSIX compliant, even `bash` breaks certain POSIX rules (they may be obscure rules but they are still broken). Again keep in mind that as I added more and more supported shells I didn't continuously sacrifice functionality. I added more shell support because adding it changed very little code, it was mainly just adding the appropriate check clause to the `whichshell.awk` script.

Again the ability to detect a sourced script's path is not something that is defined in POSIX so it is not at all surprising that the solutions for that problem is hit and miss.

The crash you mentioned sounds strange.  I still can't figure out why it should matter which compiler you used to build `libpython.dylib`.  It seems more likely that you are using two different *versions* of `libpython.dylib`.  If you build the `YouCompleteMe` stuff against `libpython.X.dylib` and then switch to using `libpython.Y.dylib` at runtime, you may end up with symbol mismatches.

Anyway, if you really need to start building clang versions of every package you use, I guess your only option (as mentioned above), is to start building your own packages using clang.  You'll have to write your own recipes and upload the resulting packages to binstar and somehow uniquely identify them so that you always get the version when you want.  (The "uniquely identify" part is a little tricky. You could give your packages unique names, or maybe special version numbers like 2.7.10.99, or possibly create a conda 'feature' called "clang".)

Testing this would be great. The easiest way is to checkout the branch and use 

```
PYTHONPATH=. conda install ...
```

P.P.S. Thanks so much for identifying the root cause of the bug, and then submitting a fix!!! Always *so* appreciated.  That bug couldn't have taken me a LONG time to find, given that it's an issue with an old version of a dependency.

so the '..' is actually indicative of using move_to_trash in a way that wasn't anticipated (the folder you trash *not* being a sub-folder of the root prefix).

it's potentially a bad security exploit too, if you get a few '..'s you can get up to the root of the system and start writing files into /usr/lib, for example.

I'm fixing the '..' now, but also keeping the mkdtemp since otherwise we'll run into failures to put things in trash since there's already a folder with the same name in there.

```python
    '$CONDA_ROOT/.condarc',
    '$CONDA_ROOT/condarc',
    '$CONDA_ROOT/condarc.d/',
    '~/.conda/.condarc',
    '~/.conda/condarc',
    '~/.conda/condarc.d/',
    '~/.condarc',
    '$CONDA_PREFIX/.condarc',
    '$CONDA_PREFIX/condarc',
    '$CONDA_PREFIX/condarc.d/',
    '$CONDARC',
)
```
I guess you are most interested in (only?) overriding the user-specific paths? I.e.,
```python
    '~/.conda/.condarc',
    '~/.conda/condarc',
    '~/.conda/condarc.d/',
    '~/.condarc',
```
Also note that there already is a `$CONDARC`, though this one is (like the other files) additive and doesn't disable the other configuration files.

> So the orm tools require flush for any insert where you want to retrieve a sequence based ID.

But if you're using the ORM, you can use the object instead of the ID. Define a `sqlalchemy.orm.relationship` between your tables and you don't need to flush - sqlalchemy will recognize the relationship between the tables and automatically do the right thing to set the ID on the Dataset_user once it's been assigned on the Dataset.  Here's an example:

```
from sqlalchemy import Column, testing, ForeignKey
from sqlalchemy.types import Integer, String
from sqlalchemy.testing import fixtures
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker, relationship

from cockroachdb.sqlalchemy import run_transaction

Base = declarative_base()


class Customer(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'customers'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    name = Column(String)


class Order(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'orders'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    customer_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('customers.id'))
    customer = relationship('Customer')
    name = Column(String)


class InsertIDTest(fixtures.TestBase):
    def teardown_method(self, method):
        Base.metadata.drop_all(testing.db)

    def setup_method(self):
        Base.metadata.create_all(testing.db)

    def test_insert(self):
        Session = sessionmaker(testing.db)

        def txn_body(session):
            customer = Customer(name="Ben")
            session.add(customer)
            order = Order(name="Foo", customer=customer)
            session.add(order)
        run_transaction(Session, txn_body)
```

This doesn't solve the problem of `flush()` not working, but it should reduce the need to use `flush()`.

I have no idea which operations you're using Curator to complete, but I highly discourage the use of load-balancers between Curator and client node(s).  Several operations can take a long time to process (particularly optimize and snapshot), which has caused many user much grief.  Your problem seems to be with delete operations, which is a different story, though.

I have seen delete operations take a toll on master nodes, but usually only when the cluster state is huge, and updating the cluster state takes enough time to cause issues.

> We have 3-5 shards per node and 9 shards per index.

If I interpret this correctly, it seems like you meant "we have 3-5 indices per node, and 9 shards per index."  If not so, this seems to indicate that you have 3 or 4 nodes per primary shard (to say nothing of replica shards), to spread them around that far.

If you meant 3-5 indices per node, and 9 shards per index, that would mean something different.  Either way, it doesn't seem like you have over 300 shards per node.  This is the point at which I would expect deletes to potentially begin affecting the cluster state.

I can't see Curator as an influencing factor in your problem here with the information you've shared.

It's good to know that the AWS plugin isn't encountering an error at the ES level.  That means that Elasticsearch is getting a legitimate 404 from Amazon.  Why Amazon is giving a 404 is still not clear.

There is no retry within Curator.  It only tries once.  See https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/api/snapshot.py#L60-L64

That means the retry is probably inside Elasticsearch and/or the AWS plugin.

* What version of Elasticsearch are you running?
* What version of the AWS plugin are you running?

`code_expired` means the device code.
2 possible causes:
1. The code is only valid for a limited period, so if you don't use the code to authenticate in the browser page, the code will expire, and adal-python's polling will fail.
2. For a period of about 7 days, particularly in Japan, the device code has server replicate issue, which caused authenticating through the browser could not succeed, again adal will fail for the same error

Please reactivate If you see new data leading to other causes

`keyring` is an optional package for token caching. It prints weird things I admit, but it's disabled on the fly where `keyring` cannot be configured or run. This does NOT block any SDK usage, see our Travis job for instance:
https://travis-ci.org/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/jobs/409164873

`keyring` will be removed as a dependency is the next version of the runtime, because we're moving to another cache system more reliable. In the meantime, you can uninstall safely `keyring` on Python 3.7, the code already ready to act without it and you won't get any `ImportError`.

This is actually I little trickier than I realized at first, because really what we want to do is to replace the files first, and *then* execute the notebooks -- but the way that nbconvert works, the notebooks and files will first be executed, then written. Additionally, I was thinking we'd copy the files over from the source directory, but the autograde step doesn't by default know what the assign step source directory is called. I need to think a bit more about the right way to do this without making it too complicated. Some thoughts that I have at the moment:

* Save the entire file contents into the database. This is suboptimal, though, if those files are large, for example.
* Make the `nbgrader_input_step_name` and `nbgrader_output_step_name` actually be one configurable option that is a dictionary with a key for each part of the pipeline. That way each step can know about the locations of all the other steps.
* Actually run nbconvert twice, first to preprocess all the notebooks and save them to the new location, then to execute them and save the grades into the database.

Following the previous suggestion and [this Stackoverflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/41782256/7649076), I wrote the following function:

```python
import json
import os.path
import re
import ipykernel
import requests
from notebook import notebookapp

def get_notebook_name():
    """
    Return the full path of the jupyter notebook.
    """
    kernel_id = re.search('kernel-(.*).json',
                          ipykernel.connect.get_connection_file()).group(1)
    servers = notebookapp.list_running_servers()
    for ss in servers:
        if ss['token']:
            token = '?token={token:s}'.format(token=ss['token'])
        else:
            token = ''
        response = requests.get('{url:s}api/sessions{token:s}'
                                ''.format(url=ss['url'], token=token))
        for nn in json.loads(response.text):
            if nn['kernel']['id'] == kernel_id:
                relative_path = nn['notebook']['path']
                return os.path.join(ss['notebook_dir'], relative_path)
```

Hi there, thanks for reporting. This is a bit complicated because numpy is
required within setup.py in order to find numpy headers to build
scikit-allel, so cannot be handled as a normal dependency. I have seen some
workarounds within setup.py for this but they add some complexity and can
cause installation of a non-optimal numpy without any blas support, so I
think it is just simpler to ask users to install numpy first before
installing scikit-allel if installing via pip (which is what the
installation docs say). Alternatively installing via conda has none of
these problems and will automatically install all dependencies.

@dougalm I tried `np.where` first actually (using `with np.errstate` and `np.isnan`), but that didn't work because the problem isn't just avoiding a `nan` when `0./0.` happens; it's that `y = ans / x` isn't valid, yet the `y` we want could be any nonzero value (which got thrown away when we multiplied by `x`, so `ans / x` doesn't recover it).

@bigblindbais Unfortunately, we can't use assignment. Also, I think any solution is going to incur a big overhead for when `x` has no nonzero values (though maybe we could just check for the fast case on the fly...).

This is indeed a known limitation of autograd - indexed assignment isn't yet supported, unfortunately.  You can usually get around this by building a list and calling np.concatenate().

Thank you for the PR! I've considered this approach before and even implemented it in very similar way.

I found that properly typing columns works well for high quality, mostly complete datasets. The main problem I found was around missing data, for example:

```python
>>> np.int8([1, 0, 1, np.nan])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer
```

Ultimately, I chose to use `pandas.to_numeric` because it's simpler and more forgiving of missing data. So I don't think this change is a good fit for Partridge out-of-box, but I will be sure to encourage stricter typing when I find the time to document custom configuration patterns.

(I'm certainly open to further discussion on this topic @danielsclint.)

I was using deadScopes to extract meaningful data from the code. Now the hurdle which I was facing was. Consider the following example:
```
class Foo:
  class Bar:
    pass
```
Now the deadScopes returned from pyflakes would be something like =>
-  module_scope

   - __len__ = {int} 1
   -  Foo {ClassDefinition}

      -  name = {Str}'Foo'
      -  source = [{ClassDef}]

-  class_scopes

   - 0 = {ClassScope}         # Class scope corresponding to Bar

     - __len__ = {int} 0

   - 1 = {ClassScope}         # Class scope corresponding to Foo

     - __len__ = {int} 1
     - Bar {ClassDefinition}  # This scope contains class Bar

       - name = {Str}'Bar'
       - source = [{ClassDef}]

So as you can see the source consists of a [cpython node {ClassDef}](http://greentreesnakes.readthedocs.io/en/latest/nodes.html#ClassDef) but I want this to be a pyflakes node(ClassDefinition in this case) to maintain consistency while traversing. I don't want to switch back to _ast nodes. Thus what I was doing, I was parsing the source object again through pyflakes `Checker` to return pyflakes node. Thus parsing a segment of code. And yes `self.scopeStack` being initialized as ModuleScope() might be a problem. Can this passed as a constructor argument. keeping the default to ModuleScope() to maintain backward compatibility?

The advantage of this is another person can use pyflakes to create more different types of scope. Say `FunctionScope` if you are traversing through a function.

Thank you for the feedback, @elyezer. I really do appreciate it. :)

I've finally written up a configuration module. See [here](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/compare/config). The code may look a bit weird if you dive straight in to the source material, so let me start off with an example of how one might use the new module:

```python
>>> from nailgun.config import ServerConfig
>>> ServerConfig(
...     'http://example.com:250',
...     auth=('Alice', 'hackme'),
...     verify=False
... ).save()
>>> ServerConfig('https://example.org').save('secondary')
>>> ServerConfig.get_labels()
(u'default', u'secondary')
>>> sc = ServerConfig.get()
>>> del sc.verify
>>> vars(sc)
{'url': u'http://example.com:250', 'auth': [u'Alice', u'hackme']}
>>> sc.save()
```

Notice how the `save` and `get` methods default to operating on a config named "default". This makes it very easy to manage a single server's connection info, yet also allows for multi-server setups.

The `ServerConfig` class has several other methods that are not demonstrated here. You can imagine what the `delete` method does. The `get_client_kwargs` method is a little less obvious:

```python
>>> from nailgun import client, config
>>> sc = config.ServerConfig(
...     'http://example.com:250',
...     auth=('Alice', 'hackme'),
...     verify=False
... )
>>> sc.get_client_kwargs()
{'verify': False, 'auth': ('Alice', 'hackme')}
>>> response = client.get(sc.url + '/api/v2', **sc.get_client_kwargs())
```

OK, so that's _how_ it works. There are a lot of interesting implementation notes, though. In no particular order:

* The configuration file can theoretically be saved in any number of formats. For example, it could be a SQLite database, a pickled dump of data, JSON, INI or plain Python code. (@elyezer) I chose not to use Python code because Python files are best suited to being read-only information stores. It's really hard to write out to Python files. INI was dropped because working with such files is kludgy: non-string data types are either hard or impossible to store (lists! augh!), they are case-insensitive by default, they lose the ability to represent certain characters like leading whitespace, and so on. A SQLite database seems like overkill and is not human-editable. A pickled dump of data is tempting due to its ability to natively store nearly any object type (and `nailgun.client` can authenticate using exotic objects like [`HTTPDigestAuth`](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/authentication/#digest-authentication), but it is again not human-readable, and so far Satellite does not seem to support such. JSON is human-editable, machine-editable, has a nice interface, supports all the common data types, and has good mindshare. (No DSLs, please.)
* `nailgun.config` does not support interfacing with Robottelo configuration files in any way. I think this is a good design choice, though. NailGun is a toolkit that is supposed to have knowledge of Satellite, but not its users. NailGun is supposed to sit here: `??? → NailGun → Satellite`. I don't want to break that nice layering of responsibilities. Instead, Satellite (and any other users of NailGun) can provide NailGun with information has necessary by creating and populating `ServerConfig` objects.
* Using double-stars (e.g. `**sc.get_client_kwargs()`) is a bit distasteful to me, but it's *really* useful. It's _so_ useful that I'm just going with it.
* The `ServerConfig` class has only one required attribute (`url`) and two optional ones (`auth` and `verify`). That's it. I've considered adding a mechanism for adding arbitrary attributes to a ServerConfig object (e.g. `headers`) but have decided against that. NailGun exists, in part, to make assumptions for you. We know that the content-type of requests is `application/json` in most cases (except when uploading files), and have baked that logic into `nailgun.client`. We know that the `auth`, `verify` and `url` options are the only three options to be used in most requests, so I've baked that knowledge into the `ServerConfig` class. And we know that for certain types of requests, other information must be passed in via e.g. the `data` argument. And for those requests, the methods on the `entities` classes suffice.
* The `ServerConfig` object is designed to interface nicely with the `entities` classes. Currently, the `entities` classes accept an optional `auth` parameter. When porting the entities over to NailGun, I'm going to drop that argument and instead add a `server_config` argument. The idea is that the default server configuration will be fetched if none is provided, or an anonymous `ServerConfig` object may be passed in. Sweet. This means that unusual, one-off server configurations can be used without polluting the global configuration file.

Having tests that depend on previous tests is a bad idea, as you often cannot guarantee the order of the tests that are run. Instead, you could create a fixture that takes care of whatever setup you need (starting a browser, logging into a website, etc) that can be used by multiple tests. There's no reason you can't have two browsers open at the same time if that's what you want.

As this is a more general pytest question, and not related to this plugin, any further discussion should happen through one of the project's [channels](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/contact.html).

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as pl

matplotlib_was_interactive = pl.isinteractive()
import cma
if not matplotlib_was_interactive:
    pl.ioff()

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 1000)
y = np.sin(x)

pl.figure()
pl.plot(x, y)
pl.show()
```

In general, `matplotlib.pyplot` shouldn't be imported outside of plotting methods, because users may want to configure the matplotlib backend, and this can't be done once `pyplot` has been imported (see pt 4 here: https://matplotlib.org/faq/usage_faq.html#what-is-a-backend).

@jbrockmendel `FutureWarning` is intended for when the semantics of something change. I think you'd use it in a case like Python 2 integer division behavior vs. Python 3 integer division behavior. Or presumably if we thought it mattered, we could raise a `FutureWarning` when two time zones of the same type compare equal but `tz1 is not tz2`, to say that semantically equal time zones of the same type will have a different semantic relationship to one another in the future. This PR deprecates an old (private) interface, so `DeprecationWarning` is more appropriate.

In any case, avoiding `DeprecationWarning` just so it's visible isn't much different from just defining a `DeprecatedPrivateInterfaceWarning` and raising that. The question is really whether we want to circumvent the default behavior of hiding deprecation warnings or not.

Yes, 1126 mostly-unused static files is... too many. (And multiply that number by 2 or more if you're using `ManifestStaticFilesStorage`.)

Note that this bundling of the full package goes against [official ckeditor recommendations](http://docs.ckeditor.com/#!/guide/dev_howtos_basic_configuration):

> Remember that one of the most important CKEditor best practices is to customize your editor build with CKBuilder and remove unneeded functionality before it makes it to your package. It is a bad practice to download the Full package and then remove plugins or buttons in your configuration. You will only be loading unnecessary stuff without any good reason.

Given that, I think it's a good idea to at least support a way for people to substitute their own build if they like.

I was able to get this working by simply removing the bundled build and instead pointing `STATICFILES_DIRS` to my own build. I got rid of 1000 unused files that way. Of course, that's not a viable long-term solution since the bundled build will be restored on each update.

But I think this approach could be officially supported fairly easily. The idea is that you would split out the ckeditor code into its own Django app (still in this same project) who's only purpose is to have a `static` directory with the ckeditor code. To get the current behavior, then, the only change is that you would put both apps in your `INSTALLED_APPS`. To substitute your own build, you just remove the static ckeditor app from `INSTALLED_APPS` and instead use `STATICFILES_DIRS` to point to your custom build.

Related to https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/698.

There seems to be an agreement on validating `only`, raising errors if names are passed that don't match a field (be it declared explicitly or added with `fields` or `additional`).

To be more explicit, both examples in OP would fail with "b is not a valid field for MySchema" or "c is not...".

I think `exclude` should be treated the same way.

(The only use case I can think of that would be broken is when using programmatically defined schemas where it might be easier for the caller to pass a list of fields names to exclude and let the schema just ignore fields that don't exist. For instance, the caller could pass `exclude=('blacklisted_field', )` to all his schemas without being sure the field actually exists in all of them. But this sounds like an edge case and I'm not convinced it is worth consideration. Besides, it might be doable with a Schema subclass. So I'm happy with an exception being raised.)

I couldn't get an instance of CalendarServer up and running and interacting with LDAP so I did the next best thing and just create a site backed by Apache and mod_auth_kerb. Here is the testing setup info and details if you wish to test it manually yourself.

# Kerberos Setup

## /etc/krb5.conf

This is my Kerberos config setup and configured to connect to the `JORDAN.LOCAL` realm.

```
# Configuration snippets may be placed in this directory as well
includedir /etc/krb5.conf.d/

includedir /var/lib/sss/pubconf/krb5.include.d/
[logging]
 default = FILE:/var/log/krb5libs.log
 kdc = FILE:/var/log/krb5kdc.log
 admin_server = FILE:/var/log/kadmind.log

[libdefaults]
 dns_lookup_realm = false
 ticket_lifetime = 24h
 renew_lifetime = 7d
 forwardable = true
 rdns = false
# default_realm = EXAMPLE.COM
 default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid}

 default_realm = JORDAN.LOCAL
[realms]
# EXAMPLE.COM = {
#  kdc = kerberos.example.com
#  admin_server = kerberos.example.com
# }

 JORDAN.LOCAL = {
  kdc = dc01.jordan.local
  admin_server = dc01.jordan.local
 }

[domain_realm]
# .example.com = EXAMPLE.COM
# example.com = EXAMPLE.COM
 jordan.local = JORDAN.LOCAL
 .jordan.local = JORDAN.LOCAL
```

## SPN

I have manually set the SPN `HTTP/centos01.jordan.local` on my test AD server

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8462645/26271564/cf7f7084-3d4a-11e7-9001-f7500204ffce.png)

## Keytab

This is my keytab setup at `etc/krb5.keytab`

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8462645/26271576/facc8a88-3d4a-11e7-988f-c5f58fe7cfa2.png)

# Apache Setup

## Apache Kerberos Config

My Kerberos config at `/etc/httpd/conf.d/auth_kerberos.conf` is configured like

```
<Directory /var/www/centos01.jordan.local>
    AuthType Kerberos
    AuthName "Kerberos Authentication"
    KrbAuthRealms JORDAN.LOCAL
    Krb5KeyTab /etc/krb5.keytab
    KrbMethodNegotiate On
    KrbMethodK5Passwd off
    KrbVerifyKDC Off
    Require user user1@JORDAN.LOCAL
</Directory>
```

I also just have a basic HTML site configured on port 80 at `/etc/httpd/sites-available/centos01.jordan.local.conf

```
<VirtualHost *:80>
    ServerName centos01.jordan.local
    ServerAlias centos01.jordan.local
    DocumentRoot /var/www/centos01.jordan.local/public_html
    ErrorLog /var/www/centos01.jordan.local/error.log
    CustomLog /var/www/centos01.jordan.local/requests.log combined
</VirtualHost>
```

# Test Script

This is the script I used to test out all 4 existing tests with the Kerberos backed website. It tests it with both Python 2.7.13 and Python 3.6.1.

I had to modify the existing tests to support both Python 2 and 3 (committed) and modify the principal to work outside of CalendarServer (just done through a sed line replacement)

```
# Change to run in root to makes things simpler
su

# Ensure any leftover tests are cleared before testing again
rm -rf /testing

# Setup testing repo
mkdir /testing
cd /testing
git clone https://github.com/jborean93/ccs-pykerberos.git -b cbt-support
cd ccs-pykerberos

# Show the current git commit hash for verification
git show --oneline -s

# Change the uri for the server test as we aren't using calendar server
sed -i '/uri = "\/principals\/"/c\    uri = "\/"' test.py

# Get kerberos ticket for Kerberos user
kinit user1@JORDAN.LOCAL

# Create virtual env for Python 2.7.13
virtualenv /testing/py27-venv -p /usr/local/bin/python2.7
source /testing/py27-venv/bin/activate
python --version

# Install new ccs-pykerberos library with CBT support
pip install .

# Run tests for Python 2.7.13
python test.py -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local service
python test.py -u user1 -p Password01 -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local -r JORDAN.LOCAL basic

python test.py -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local -r JORDAN.LOCAL gssapi
python test.py -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local -h centos01.jordan.local -i 80 server

# Create virtual env for Python 3.6.1 and run tests
deactivate
virtualenv /testing/py36-venv -p /usr/local/bin/python3.6
source /testing/py36-venv/bin/activate
python --version

pip install .

python test.py -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local service
python test.py -u user1 -p Password01 -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local -r JORDAN.LOCAL basic

python test.py -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local -r JORDAN.LOCAL gssapi
python test.py -s HTTP@centos01.jordan.local -h centos01.jordan.local -i 80 server
```

> Is the pvlib community interested in using development branches for larger
> features and changes? The idea is to get relatively large proposals in the
> hands of more people before they're merged. For example, #124
> <https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/pull/124> is a large feature
> addition and #151 <https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/pull/151> breaks
> the existing API, so they require a 0.x version change to merge instead of
> only a 0.x.y change. More importantly, I am reluctant to merge large
> changes without more significant user feedback.
>
> But critical user feedback is harder to get if it requires navigating the
> git maze. *If* you're comfortable using git then it's very easy (and
> powerful) to checkout another user's PR to try for yourself, but if you're
> newish to git and using GitHub Desktop or similar then it's somewhere
> between a mystery and impossible to do that. I think (hope?) that newish
> users of git and GitHub Desktop can switch branches of a single remote
> repository without too much trouble.
>
> How would this work in practice? All features would still start out as PRs
> against master. Depending on the scope, we may decide to add it as a new
> branch once the code is useable. One of the maintainers would have to
> complete that step. The original PR can then be closed, and a new PR can be
> opened from the development branch to the master branch. The original
> developer can still submit his/her own PRs against the development branch.
> The maintainers can quickly merge these PRs with abandon since they don't
> impact master. Once everything is good, the development branch can be
> merged into master and deleted.

PyPI is the official index (https://pypi.python.org/pypi). It's where you should upload the package. The typical way to do this is to use `python setup.py sdist`, `python setup.py register`, and `python setup.py upload` (sdist means source tarball; it's also possible to build binary distributions called wheels, but I would worry about figuring that out after you get everything else working). 

`pip` is the tool that installs packages from PyPI. So most people will install the Python bindings with `pip install z3`. They can also download the source from PyPI manually and run `python setup.py install`. 

PyPy is an alternative implementation of Python that is focused on performance. The only relationship it has to PyPI is that people often (mis)pronounce PyPI as "PyPy". 

https://www.pythonpackages.com/ looks like it is just a little tool that gathers data from PyPI. It isn't necessary to use (I've never even heard of it before). 

What you want to do here is make sure that `python setup.py sdist` works and creates a tarball that can be installed on its own.

Before anybody wastes any more time on this, can we please do a rought cost/benefit calculation?

I was under the impression that this contribution should be self-contained, non-disruptive, and basically @delcypher's pet project that will stay invisible to the end-user until they actively want to use it. It seems that the current strategy is to partially or completely replace the existing build system. And at the current time and state of affairs I'm strongly opposed to this, because it will most definitely be a huge load of time and effort for all of us, which nobody will get back in any kind of benefit. 

Here are some thoughts:

If we wanted Makefiles in every directory, we would have done so a long time ago, and we would have made them nmake-compatible. In retrospect that may perhaps have been less effort overall, but at the current state I see no reason to change our mind, now that all the kinks have been worked out. 

Supporting and maintaining two independent builds systems is a truly terrible idea, for all people that are involved. I am open to discussion about replacing the existing system, but that will have to be much better organized and there will have to be a lot of discussion about what system we think is the best choice. I would much rather see that time and effort to go into refactoring of the existing system to make maintenace less painful, which, at the same time, would be much less disruptive for users as well.

CMake is a _great_ system if you need to build, test, and publish on multiple platforms. More than 99% of our users do not have that requirement. The number of people who do have this requirement is 2 (@NikolajBjorner and myself), and that set of people is perfectly happy with the current system. And further, they have about a 5-year investment of fixing build problems on exotic platforms in the current system. Much of this will have to be re-discovered and re-fixed. I can't speak for @NikolajBjorner, but personally I am not in any way keen on going down that route.

Prospective future users could be found in the set of people that maintain our APIs (currently the same 2), because they have to make sure their APIs work on all platforms. But, at the same time this is also the most complicated part and so far has received no attention as far as I can tell. 

Adding yet another tool in the build chain is completely unnecessary complication for "newbie" users. Many of our users are not Comp Sci students and they do not run Ubuntu. If we have to ask them to compile the latest-bugfix version from the master branch, everything will have to go through without a hitch, because they won't have the knowledge or skills to fix any problems along the way. And yes, some of them will have trouble downloading CMake and installing it, and they will have no clue what's going on when they run the magic commands that we tell them to run.

If we were to decide to replace the existing system, then CMake would not be my first choice of tool to use. (E.g., why don't they publish packages for BSDs? Current platforms on my todo list are Raspian, Solaris, OpenBSD. Will we have to give people a tutorial on how to compile CMake first?) Also, I would much rather just have Makefiles in every directory and ./configure scripts, and perhaps a static .vcxproj for Visual Studio. 

So, yes, I do appreciate @delcypher's enthusiams about this project, but at this point in time, from my point of view, it's just not cost-effective. This is OK if it stays invisible, but not otherwise. So at this point, I won't merge this PR, but I'm not the only one with write permissions, and if the others think there are benefits that I can't see, then I'm happy to be overruled.

There is a bug where unicorn is failing to notice that it's working with symbolic data. I'll investigate this. In the meantime, some background:

The point of unicorn is that a step will often step more than a single basic block. When you have unicorn enabled and say step it's actually stepped forward as far as possible. When you pass num_inst, you're implicitly disabling unicorn for that step.

The closest out-of-the-box feature is `LAZY_SOLVES`. Unless a condition is concretely false, that option will cause the path to continue to be executed until an actual unsat error is encountered. It's already on by default.

One other way you can do this, currently, is to have an exploration technique in which you check the unsat stash (make sure the path group is created with save_unsat) and reinject them into the active stash. However, since the guard condition is added to the constraint set, it could make your state unsat. With an unsat state, there's no reasonable way to, for example, resolve symbolic writes, since all constraint solves fail. You'd have to remove the last constraint to maintain "satness". That's a bit of a hack, since the states won't be completely consistent afterwards.

An alternate way to do this would be to set up a SimInspect hook in the code that deals with categorizing successor states (in SimRun). That code is currently being refactored, but afterwards, that feature should be fairly easy to add. It'd be fairly equivalent (and have the same drawbacks) as the exploration technique method, though.

So it looks like this is caused by an overapproximation of the switch jump in the middle of main(). It doesn't quite get the bounds on the index correct, so it starts treating the data after the pointer table as pointers, and since they obviously aren't targets inside main(), it treats them as inter-function jumps instead of intra-function, and so they get added to the callgraph. As a quick test of this, you can look at the data associated with the edges in the callgraph linking to these absurd targets, and noting that each of them have `{'type': 'transition'}` instead of `{'type': 'call'}`.

Sure:

```
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,012 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | started emulation at 0x4012f9 (1000000 steps)
```

Here, angr diverts to unicorn engine, beginning with the basic block at 0x4012f9. The maximum step count is set to 1000000, so if execution stays in Unicorn for 1000000 blocks, it'll automatically pop out (to avoid hanging in an infinite loop).

---------------------------------------

```
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,014 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x401000, 0x401fff], 5 (symbolic)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,016 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x7fffffffffe0000, 0x7fffffffffeffff], 3 (symbolic)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,019 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x6010000, 0x601ffff], 3
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,022 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x602000, 0x602fff], 3 (symbolic)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,023 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x400000, 0x400fff], 5
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,025 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x7000000, 0x7000fff], 5
```

angr performs lazy mapping of data that is accessed by unicorn engine, as it is accessed. 0x401000 is the page of instructions that it is executing, 0x7fffffffffe0000 is the stack, and so on. Some of these pages are symbolic, meaning that they contain at least some data that, when accessed, will cause execution to abort out of Unicorn.

---------------------------------------

```
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,037 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | finished emulation at 0x7000080 after 3 steps: STOP_STOPPOINT
```

Execution stays in Unicorn for 3 basic blocks (a computational waste, considering the required setup), after which it reaches a simprocedure location and jumps out to execute the simproc in angr.

---------------------------------------

```
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,076 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | started emulation at 0x40175d (1000000 steps)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,077 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x401000, 0x401fff], 5 (symbolic)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,079 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x7fffffffffe0000, 0x7fffffffffeffff], 3 (symbolic)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,081 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | mmap [0x6010000, 0x601ffff], 3
```

After the simprocedure, execution jumps back into Unicorn.

---------------------------------------

```
WARNING | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,082 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | fetching empty page [0x0, 0xfff]
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,103 | simuvex.plugins.unicorn | finished emulation at 0x401777 after 1 steps: STOP_EXECNONE
```

Execution bounces out of Unicorn almost right away because the binary accessed the zero-page.

---------------------------------------

```
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,120 | simuvex.engines.unicorn_engine | not enough runs since last unicorn (100)
INFO    | 2017-02-25 08:19:48,125 | simuvex.engines.unicorn_engine | not enough runs since last unicorn (99)
```

To avoid thrashing in and out of Unicorn (which is expensive), we have cooldowns (attributes of the `state.unicorn` plugin) that wait for certain conditions to hold (i.e., no symbolic memory accesses for X blocks) before jumping back into unicorn when a unicorn run is aborted due to anything but a simprocedure or syscall. Here, the condition it's waiting for is for 100 blocks to be executed before jumping back in.
Note on failing testcase: it’s a CLE bug that seems to have snuck in for the grub example, not from this PR.

I'll look more closely at what you're trying to do, but without any knowledge of the intent of your code it looks like you might have an off-by-one problem when you try to step paths to a given address.

```python
        while len(p.successors) == 1:
            # Find the target function in a trace
            if func1.addr in [x for x in p.addr_trace]:
                #print '**%d steps: %s' % (len(prev.addr_trace), [hex(x) for x in prev.addr_trace])
                base_state = prev.state
                print ">>> (1-1) STATE BEFORE CALLING FUNC1"
                util.pp_state(prev.state, stack_bytes=32, arch='x86')
                #print '**', prev.state.se.constraints
                break
            prev = p
            p = p.successors[0]
            p.step()
```

This will actually step the path a single basic block past the start of func1, since `addr_trace` is purely historical data about where the path has been, and does not include the current address. What you want is to compare against `p.addr`.

As an additional side-note, you can use `p.addr_trace.hardcopy` instead of `[x for x in p.addr_trace]` :)

Hello @fouvy 

In a lack of better public documentation I can offer a list of key differences.

First of all, both libraries will work with the SR300 depth camera, but Intel RealSense SDK 2016 R2 is in process of being deprecated and we encourage customers to consider SDK 2.0.

That said, 

* Intel RealSense SDK **2016 R2** includes several computer-vision middlewares tailored for the depth camera. The algorithms are closed-source proprietary IP.
* Intel RealSense SDK **2.0** does not include any computer-vision middlewares. We will continue to provide samples and guides for integration with existing 3rd-party libraries and technologies ([like OpenCV](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/master/wrappers/opencv)), publish white-papers and end-to-end tutorials ([example here](https://software.intel.com/en-us/articles/build-an-autonomous-mobile-robot-with-the-intel-realsense-camera-ros-and-sawr)), and encourage community involvement. 
> As a result of extra functionality Intel RealSense SDK **2016 R2** has much larger footprint

* Intel RealSense SDK **2016 R2** was only validated on Windows 10, not including the later Microsoft updates (like the Creators Update). To the best of my knowledge it will not work on Windows 8, and certainly not on Linux or Mac OS. In addition, it requires relatively modern Intel CPU and will not run on ARM. 
* Intel RealSense SDK **2.0** is currently being tested on modern versions of Windows 10 and Ubuntu 16, but it will also work on earlier versions of Windows (Windows 8 and with some modifications also Windows 7), Mac OS, most Linux distributions and rooted Android phones. 

> Non-Intel specific optimizations are relatively low in our backlog, but such enhancements would be welcomed and we hope to offer more of them down the line

While the feature-set of the SDK had to be reduced, we are trying to increase the overall number of ways our best devices can be used.

Hi again :)

I've opened PR #776  to use the alignment processing block of the SDK.
Also, I've composed a small example which tries to imitate the `rs-align` example (i.e background removal).
> NOTE: I'm not an expert in `numpy` and I'm probably masking the color image incorrectly

```py
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
# Create a pipeline
pipeline = rs.pipeline()
#Create a config and configure the pipeline to stream
#  different resolutions of color and depth streams
config = rs.config()
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 640, 360, rs.format.z16, 30)
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.color, 640, 480, rs.format.bgr8, 30)
# Start streaming
profile = pipeline.start(config)
# Getting the depth sensor's depth scale (see rs-align example for explanation)
depth_sensor = profile.get_device().first_depth_sensor()
depth_scale = depth_sensor.get_depth_scale()
print "depth_scale : " , depth_scale
# Create an align object
align = rs.align(rs.stream.color)
# Streaming loop
try:
    while True:
        # Get frameset of color and depth
        frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
        depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
        print "Depth Resolution: " , depth_frame.get_width() , "x" , depth_frame.get_height()
        # Align the depth frame to color frame
        proccessed = align.proccess(frames)
        # Get aligned frames
        aligned_depth_frame = proccessed.get_depth_frame()
        print "Aligned Depth Resolution: " , aligned_depth_frame.get_width() , "x" , aligned_depth_frame.get_height()
        color_frame = proccessed.get_color_frame()
        if not aligned_depth_frame or not color_frame:
            continue
        depth_image = np.asanyarray(aligned_depth_frame.get_data())
        color_image = np.asanyarray(color_frame.get_data())
        # My lousy attempt to perform background removal
        depth_image_3d = np.repeat(depth_image[:, :, np.newaxis], 3, axis=2)
        depth_image_3d = depth_image_3d * depth_scale
        bg_removed = np.where(depth_image_3d > 1, 0, color_image)
        # Render images
        depth_colormap = cv2.applyColorMap(cv2.convertScaleAbs(depth_image, None, 0.5, 0), cv2.COLORMAP_JET)
        images = np.hstack((bg_removed, color_image, depth_colormap))
        cv2.namedWindow('RealSense', cv2.WINDOW_AUTOSIZE)
        cv2.imshow('RealSense', images)
        cv2.waitKey(1)
finally:
    pipeline.stop()
```

@sccrthlt I assume the update formulas you mention are the one from Sutton, Barto, p. 274 (the box titled "One-step actor-critic (episodic)", (or with slightly modified notation algorithm S3 from the Mnih et al paper). Note that in their notation parameters w are used for value function, and theta - for policy, but in principle there can be overlap between w and theta parameters.  
Consider the loss function 
   L(w, theta) = value_loss(w) + policy_gradient_loss(theta)
Then (I really wish github started supporting latex :)
dL/dw = dvalue_loss(w)/dw,
dL/dtheta = dpolicy_gradient_loss/dtheta

so the update equations (if the simple SGD is used, without extra fancifications such as adam or momentum) are:
w   <--   w - alpha * dvalue_loss / dw
theta   <--   theta - alpha * dpolicy_gradient_loss/dtheta = theta + alpha * advantage * d (log pi) / d theta
(because policy_gradient_loss = - advantage * log pi) 
which is the same as the update formulas you provided. If some of the parameters are shared, then the corresponding update will be a sum of both value gradient update and gradient of log pi update - whether computed through gradient of the combined loss or as a result of two updates. 
I 'll close this issue for now, however, @sccrthlt, please re-open if the explanation above is not clear or does not address your question.

The current version of Connection paginates using the index within the list you're currently at, and calculates the total count to evaluate whether there is another page after the current one. Cursor pagination identifies ordering data from the field, and loads "the first n after the ordering value x". By loading n+1 items, you know whether there is at least one item afterwards, but you have no idea whether there is 1 or 10000. This style of query is also much more efficient for the database when loading much later pages.

[More details of the approach in a blog post by David Cramer](http://cra.mr/2011/03/08/building-cursors-for-the-disqus-api)

1. use default graph scope
```
for i in range(30):
     with tensorflow.Graph().as_default():
     .............
```
2. We can modify fit() method to not to take array inputs and rather it should accept feed to placeholders.

Yep, the caching worked for me -- but the SAML assertion I'm getting back from Google has:

```xml
<!-- ... ->
<saml2:SubjectConfirmationData ... NotOnOrAfter="...">
<!-- ... ->
<saml2:Conditions ... NotOnOrAfter="...">
```
which, according to my slightly high-level knowledge of the SAML2 spec, means:

* `SubjectConfirmationData` time data specifies the time window during which the assertion can be supplied to the SP (in this case, AWS)
* `Conditions` time data specifies the time window that the SP should treat the session as valid (in other words, the maximum issued duration of the STS credentials)

According to the spec, the `SubjectConfirmationData` has to be within the `Conditions` time window.

Unless I'm mistaken, this means that caching the assertions is only of limited use, since Google issues its response with both `SubjectConfirmationData` and `Conditions` with a 10-minute window (5 minutes prior to the authentication time and 5 minutes after, to account for clock skew).

This concurs with my testing -- the cached assertion was usable to get new credentials, but only until 5 minutes after my initial authentication.

Is there a way to tell Google to issue assertions with a longer time window? I haven't been able to find one yet ...

@mayli I'm sorry for strong language. I don't know design of your application, it may be OK considering how you use threads. In general case we can't include it in httplib2 because almost always it will be used in wrong way.

Problems:

* **prevents connection reuse** - you start a thread and make request which opens new connection, later you start new thread and make request to same server, but now it must open new connection instead of using existing idle one
* easy to overwhelm remote servers with excess concurrent requests causing DoS for them and IP blacklist for you
* hard to reason how much local resources your program is consuming

As of 2017-08 `httplib2.Http` object promise to have at most 1 outgoing connection to each network location (scheme:host:port). So developer is able to control number of connections per remote server (prevent DoS) and overall outgoing connections (to account local resources). With connection pool per thread you basically multiple those numbers by number of threads. With green threads usually there is either no thread limit or it's very high, thus you get outgoing connections out of control.

@temoto I got those two possible approaches for this issue
1. have a toggle in `__init__` to enable/disable sharing between threads
2. provide a optional kwarg to customize the computing of `conn_key`, eg `conn_key_func=None`

approach 1 is easier and could fit most use cases (at least works for me)
approach 2 is more flexible and could also be implemented as an extra method to override (more oop), but this will introduce an extra function all per requests (more cpu cycles slightly)

```

    constexpr arg_t<T> operator=(const T &value) const { return {name, value}; }
```

you're definitely using version 1.8.1. (The latest version implements this assignment differently and in a different location.)

If you're using `pip`, make sure to enable pre-release versions when installing packages: `pip install --pre pybind11`. The default `pip install` will only download stable releases (1.8.1) which is most likely the issue.

BTW The bug itself was fixed in #244 -- 0 was implicitly cast to a pointer.

With #394 merged, you'll get that error now if you do `obj.cast<std::string &>()` or `py::cast<std::string &>(obj)` (or call something that invokes one of those).  What was happening before, though, is that you'd get an std::string& reference, but it's a reference to an object that has already been deleted: we only have an std::string instance for the duration of the `cast` call.

You can see this yourself with the following code, using either v1.8.1, or master with just the first commit of #394 applied, using this code:
```C++
#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>
PYBIND11_PLUGIN(t) {
    namespace py = pybind11;
    py::module m("t");
    m.def("foo", [](py::object o) { std::cout << o.cast<std::string &>() << "\n"; });
    return m.ptr();
}
```
and this python:
```Python
import t
t.foo("asdf" * 10)
```
That gives me output of some random garbage, followed by a few (but not all) of the `asdf`'s; here's the output run through `xxd`:

```
00000000: 884b e87d 897f 0000 c07b 7a02 0000 0000  .K.}.....{z.....
00000010: 6173 6466 6173 6466 6173 6466 6173 6466  asdfasdfasdfasdf
00000020: 6173 6466 6173 6466 0a                   asdfasdf.
```
and here's the output if I drop the `&` from the cast:
```
00000000: 6173 6466 6173 6466 6173 6466 6173 6466  asdfasdfasdfasdf
00000010: 6173 6466 6173 6466 6173 6466 6173 6466  asdfasdfasdfasdf
00000020: 6173 6466 6173 6466 0a                   asdfasdf.
```

So this is definitely correct *now*, and was definitely not correct before.

I agree it'd be good to fix this. We used to add a lot of aliases like this, but we don't do so anymore as I think it often just complicates the CLI.

Unfortunately, it's a bit cumbersome to fix this because our current argument parser doesn't retain this information. For example, `--staging` and `--test-cert` both modify the value of `staging` in the parsed configuration and no information is retained about what flag set this value. To fix this, we'd have to write our own code to (re)parse the command line to determine which flag set it and we may have to run this code every time we want to return an error string like this.

Things get a lot more complicated if we also want to do this for values set in a [configuration file](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#configuration-file).

This might be a lot easier if we resolve https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/4493 first.

A quick exception dissection: looks like the exception was raised in `init_save_key` that calls [`make_key`][1], which is supposed to return a string but, in this case, returns a byte string. We then try to write this byte-string into a file object opened with 'w', which means it expects an encoded string (e.g unicode), and fails as expected.

[1]: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/crypto_util.py#L207
[2]: https://github.com/certbot/certbot#system-requirements

Solving the same problem with Gurobi I get the same duals for `C[8]` to `C[15]` while `C[3] = 1` and `C[4] = 2` (all other duals are "positive" `0.0`). Note that when you take adjacent pairs of constraints and sum up the absolute values of the difference of their duals you then get `14`, the dual optimal value.

For completeness, here is the original test case written for PICOS. Both primal and dual tests pass for all of CPLEX, CVXOPT, GLPK, Gurobi, MOSEK and SMCP while for my work in progress reimplementation of SCIP only the primal test passes (the old SCIP implementation did not retrieve any duals):
```
    def setUp(self):
        # Data.
        c = picos.new_param("c", cvxopt.matrix([1,2,3,4,5]))
        A = picos.new_param("A", [
            cvxopt.matrix([1,0,0,0,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([0,0,2,0,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([0,0,0,2,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([1,0,0,0,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([1,0,2,0,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([0,1,1,1,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([1,2,0,0,0]).T,
            cvxopt.matrix([1,0,3,0,1]).T
        ])
        self.n = n = len(A)
        self.m = m = c.size[0]

        # Primal problem.
        self.P = P = picos.Problem()
        self.u = u = P.add_variable("u", m)
        P.set_objective("max", c|u)
        self.C = P.add_list_of_constraints(
            [abs(A[i]*u) < 1 for i in range(n)], "i", "[n]")

        # Dual problem.
        self.D = D = picos.Problem()
        self.z = z = [D.add_variable("z[{}]".format(i)) for i in range(n)]
        self.mu = mu = D.add_variable("mu", n)
        D.set_objective("min", 1|mu)
        D.add_list_of_constraints(
            [abs(z[i]) < mu[i] for i in range(n)], "i", "[n]")
        D.add_constraint(
            picos.sum([A[i].T * z[i] for i in range(n)], "i", "[n]") == c)

        self.skipUnsupported(P, D)

    def testPrimalSolution(self):
        self.primalSolve(self.P)
        self.expectObjective(self.P, 14.0)

    def testDualSolution(self):
        self.dualSolve(self.P)
        for i in range(self.n):
            self.z[i].set_value(self.C[i].dual[1] - self.C[i].dual[0])
        self.mu.set_value(cvxopt.matrix([abs(z_i.value) for z_i in self.z]))
        self.expectObjective(self.D, 14.0)
```

Commit 276b2f18bcbac4adc4f5ad36353f12b8b03cb8fa renamed `CancelTransfer` to `RefundTransfer` and added the following semantics:

- for nodes that are initiating a transfer and receive a `RefundTransfer`, generate a new secret and hashlock and tries a new path
- for nodes that are mediating a transfer and receive a `RefundTransfer`, register the transfer to get the refund and try a new path with the same hashlock
- for nodes that are mediating a transfer and don't have avaiable paths left, create a `RefundTransfer`, send to the previous node and register a `LogListenerTask` if the previous node reveals the secret only in the blockchain.

For the UDP messages we can use a prefix encoding, adding the data length as a prefix to the data itself. I'm not sure how that will play out though, because the code is allocating the buffer before the data is encoded.

For the REST API it may be nicer to allow for arbitrary json, that raises a compatiblity problem though, if a different client uses the format directly. One solution is to add a prefix with the mime encoding of the data.

That test case appears to _also_ be a test case of [F.5 ‘path’ element implementation notes](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG11/single-page.html#implnote-PathElementImplementationNotes).  Said section says that any path should be rendered up to (but excluding) the first path command that is in error.  This means that the (invalid) command:

```svg
<path d="M200,200 h-25 a25,2501 025,-25 z" fill="red"/>
```

Should be rendered just as `M200,200 h-25` (and then skipping the `a` and `z` command).  Should scour aggressively optimize the broken path away or should it "ignore" the path (possible with a warning)?

To satisfy your requirements we should use datatype which behaves better than floats or ints. Such datatype exists (i.e. python decimal https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/decimal.html), but is not supported by numpy.

What I propose is to upcast from `int16` to `int32` (and `int8` to `int16`) **when reading**. Integer cubes are used for CT scans and doses and do not store values larger than few thousands (when read). Upcasting to `int32` should practically solve most of overflow/underflow problems. Moreover it is no more than few lines of code.

I agree that lazy upcasting (upon arithmetic operation) is much harder to maintain, but more elegant. This is why I consider it as a second choice.

In my opinion all mathematical operations between floats and ints are correctly handled by numpy and there is no need to convert to floats.

There is also another issue when converting everything to float32, see **set_data_type** method https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/master/pytrip/cube.py#L841.
What I guess from it is that user could have a TRiP98 cube with 32bit integers. Those integers cannot be converted to float32, for them float64 is more appropriate type.

I can write some proof-of-concept code if you want.

It's not really worth the effort.

Python 2.6 has been EOL for four years and no longer receives security updates. Many other important Python libraries are also dropping it, and it's little used.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1324225/33706416-c48c2d3a-db3c-11e7-98d5-89862db51984.png)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CPython#Version_history

Here's the pip installs for marisa-trie from PyPI for the last month (via `pypinfo --percent --pip marisa-trie pyversion`), showing virtually no usage.

| python_version | percent | download_count |
| -------------- | ------: | -------------: |
| 2.7            |   59.3% |          3,048 |
| 3.5            |   20.9% |          1,074 |
| 3.6            |   14.3% |            734 |
| 3.4            |    5.4% |            279 |
| 2.6            |    0.1% |              4 |

This shows there's very little demand for maintaining 2.6. Anyone stuck on 2.6 can pin to the last version that did. They will be doing this already for other libraries. 

You will be able to use modern features of 2.7+.

There's an argument to be made that maintaining support for 2.6 is bad for the community as a whole, see https://snarky.ca/stop-using-python-2-6/ and 
https://www.curiousefficiency.org/posts/2015/04/stop-supporting-python26.html

The stdin option mentioned by @bastimeyer and @beardypig seems to work well. I put together a small script, it could still use a switch the the VLC buffer instead of streamlink, but it's great for me so far:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Save and buffer a stream to a file, while allowing for asynchronous viewing

if [ "$#" -ne 1 ]; then
	echo "Usage: watch.sh [url]"
	exit 1
fi

URL="$1"
channel=$(basename "$URL")
datestamp=$(date +%Y-%m-%d--%H-%M-%M)
filename="$channel-$datestamp.mkv"

streamToPipe() {
	CMD+=(
		# Allow for long pausing? Maybe?
		--ringbuffer-size 400M

		# Only watch when primary
		--twitch-disable-hosting

		# Closer to live, just pause for a sec if it buffers
		--hls-live-edge 1

		# Output stream to stdout
		-O
	)

	streamlink "${CMD[@]}" $URL best
}

# The natively-downloaded video has a problem with keyframes (?)
# This will mean that scrubbing in VLC will result in a gray smear.
# Easily fixed by ffmpeg!
fixup() {
	# Switch to this version to just save anyway
	# tee "$filename"

	tee >(ffmpeg -i - -c:v copy -c:a copy "$filename")
}

# VLC is not in my system path. You may want to adjust or remove this part.
vlc="/c/Program Files/VideoLAN/VLC/vlc"

set -x
streamToPipe | fixup | "$vlc" -
```

Maybe this will be useful to someone, maybe not. Improvements accepted :)

I think the core change to implement this would be to add an instrument hook for "task is at a cancellation point". Possibly the cleanest way to do this would be to call it from `yield_if_cancelled` and `yield_indefinitely`, so that the instrument gets called inside task context and can do things like inspect the stack.

The other way to do it would be to put the instrument hook in `yield_if_cancelled` and in the run loop where we receive the `yield_indefinitely` yield. But this leads to pretty different calling contexts (can potentially be handled by saying that you always have to fetch the task object and interrogate its coroutine object – though, does that work properly when a coroutine is running?), and worse, it means that if an instrument issues a cancellation in `yield_if_cancelled` then it will get called again a moment later when we actually yield.

Though... that's... actually what happens in both designs. Ugh. I guess we could give `yield_if_cancelled` its own unique trap, that yields the same object as `yield_indefinitely` but doesn't have the instrument hook.

This all might require a bit of code rearranging, since `yield_indefinitely` currently lives in a different file from the instrument logic.

Possibly this is also an argument for having some kind of first-class support for `yield_indefinitely_no_cancellation`, since `run_in_worker_thread` in particular uses `yield_indefinitely` without it being a cancellation point, and it could lead to a bit of silliness if we inject a cancel there. In particular: if a test wants to check that all cancel points actually cause `Cancelled` to propagate out, then this would mess that up.

You can do that in a second step using [loompy.combine](http://linnarssonlab.org/loompy/fullapi/loompy.html#loompy.loompy.combine)
Notice that the function will combine the files without the need of loading the full matrixes into memory.

If you want to do this from the command line you can simply put together a simple script, something like the following should do:

```python
import sys
import loompy

loompy.combine(sys.argv[1:-1], sys.argv[-1])
# usage: script.py file1.loom file2.loom file3.loom merged.loom
```
